There should be one test fixture that starts/stops solr and another fixture that configures collective.solr.

The test fixture should use a "test" Solr core, otherwise tests will clear the real solr data (on your local machine).

We also have to make sure that solr is properly stopped, even if we just run a single test.

The fixture should be usable for both "unit" tests against the solr configuration and for integration, functional and robot-framework-based acceptance tests.

collective.solr should use that test fixture to replace the alltests script.

Todo:

- [ ] Add SolrFixture (for Unit Tests)
- [ ] Add SolrPloneFixture (for Integration, Functional, Acceptance Tests)
- [ ] Document test fixtures
- [ ] Make sure test teardown works when running single tests
- [ ] Make tests use a test solr core
- [ ] Make collective.solr use the new fixtures and replace the alltests script.

issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is the expected behavior, but when I search in ncmpcpp, search results that are albums show up only as URIs, e.g.:

Should they show up as album names instead?

There seems to be some inconsistencies with respect to the `encoding` argument on `String` objects.  For example (in py3):
```
>>> import colander
>>> s = colander.String('utf-8')
>>> s.serialize(None, 123)
'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, '123')
b'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, str(123))
b'123'
```
in py2:
```
>>> import colander
>>> s = colander.String('utf-8')
>>> s.serialize(None, 123)
u'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, '123')
'123'
>>> s.serialize(None, unicode(123))
'123'
```
Basically, if you pass something that isn't an instance of one of the python string types you can bypass the encoding step.

The docs say:
> A non-Unicode input value to ``serialize`` is converted to a
> Unicode using the encoding (``unicode(value, encoding)``);
> subsequently the Unicode object is reeencoded to a ``str``
> object using the encoding and returned.

This seems to be a regression as it appears it did work properly in previous versions.
Any possiblity to add https://github.com/Qix-/better-exceptions into this? would be good?

issue_title: Interface still reflects cached assignments even after assigned Network object is deleted
issue_body:
When a `Network` address is assigned to an `Interface` and the underlying `Network` object is deleted from the database, the `Interface._addresses_cache` and `Interface._networks_cache` are no updated. This is an inconsistency that needs to be addressed.

Possible solutions:
- A `Network` object cannot be deleted as long as it has an existing `Assignment` relationship to an `Interface`
- Deleting a `Network` with an `Assignment` in place also triggers an update to the `Interface` to refresh its cached relationships.

The Selenium 2.53.6 is two years old. The main reason to support Selenium 2.53.6 is the compatibility with older (I think it was Firefox 42 and older). issue_title: `Wait For Condition` should validate that condition contains `return`
issue_body:
`Wait For Condition` requires the condition to return the value to be evaluated. For example, I just used

    Wait For Condition    document.title == 'Example'

and it didn't work. After reading the docs I changed it to this working variant

    Wait For Condition    return document.title == 'Example'

The keyword should check that there is `return` in the condition. This change can cause problems if the `return` isn't mandatory, but AFAIK it it.

      33

If we create a tiny bounding box using the point coordinate, we get the correct results.

issue_title: pipenv --three install produces correct results where --two does not
issue_body:
Be sure to check the existing issues, both open and closed.

Describe the issue briefly here.

##### Describe your environment

1. Linux
1. Python version: `3.6.4` and `2.7.9`
1. Pipenv version: `11.10.0`

##### Expected result

I expected the wtforms package contents to be correct.

##### Actual result

It appears the wtforms package contents was the latest version overridden by the specified version.

##### Steps to replicate

This is easiest to replicate with docker where the environment is clean:

```sh
docker run -it --rm python bash

pip install pipenv==11.10.0

export PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT=1

# Test python3
mkdir /test-python3 && cd /test-python3
pipenv install --three wtforms==1.0.5

# Notice files line up with wtforms==1.0.5
# (https://pypi.python.org/packages/fb/9d/9dcdb7a9a19301c0cc04eca1aa8c60da50d97156f3c10e24519eb77202e1/WTForms-1.0.5.zip)
ls -la .venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/wtforms/

# Test python2
mkdir /test-python2 && cd /test-python2
pipenv install --two wtforms==1.0.5

# Notice it appears the files from wtforms==2.1
# (https://pypi.python.org/packages/bf/91/2e553b86c55e9cf2f33265de50e052441fb753af46f5f20477fe9c61280e/WTForms-2.1.zip)
# were overriden by the files from wtforms==1.0.5
ls -la .venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/wtforms/
```

I've recently started using pipenv. I like it, but I miss having `pip show` around. Doing `pip shell ; pip show PKG_NAME` makes me unhappy.

I've started a branch to add this command, although it occurs to me to ask whether this is considered a bad feature to add for some reason, such as because there's another canonical way to get this info that I didn't know about or something.

My WIP PR is #1461. I do plan to make it less crappy, although not before hearing that the basic idea is welcome.

##### Describe your environment

1. Arch Linux
1. Python version: `3.6.4`
1. Pipenv version: `9.0.3`

issue_title: local editable version still in use after `pipenv uninstall '<hash>'`
issue_body:
Be sure to check the existing issues, both open and closed.

* You have the latest version of a dependency that's on an index like PyPI installed using `pipenv install dep`. 
* You `import dep`, and check `dep.__file__` and it returns something along the lines of `'/users/<user>/.virtualenvs/madness-HMebEsp1/Lib/site-packages/dep'`, which is good. 
* You want to use `dep` with some custom features, so you clone it onto your disk and use `pipenv install -e /path/to/local/dep` to play with it. 
* You `import dep` and `dep.__file__`  and you get `path/to/local/dep/dep/__init__.py` which makes you happy. The Pipefile now has: `"8c9b980" = {path = "path/to/local/dep", editable = true}` added to it.
* You want to revert back to using the PyPI version because your gave up on your feature, so you uninstall it using `pipenv uninstall '<hash>'` , where <hash> is `8c9b980`. 
* You `import dep` and `dep.__file__`  and you STILL get `path/to/local/dep/dep/__init__.py`, even though `"8c9b980" = {path = "path/to/local/dep", editable = true}` has been removed from the Pipefiles. This makes you sad :disappointed:. 

... or should it? is this by design? Am I missing something about how these editable installs are supposed to be used?
 
##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type windows
1. Python version: `$ python -V` 2.7.12
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version`  9.0.3

to see `dep.__file__` return something like `'/users/<user>/.virtualenvs/madness-HMebEsp1/Lib/site-packages/dep` just like it did before the local editable version was installed.

see above.

see above.

issue_title: including non-data files without MANIFEST file
issue_body: There is a [pre-defined list of files](https://docs.python.org/3.4/distutils/sourcedist.html#specifying-the-files-to-distribute) that get included in the source release. Beyond this, if they are not data files such as `NEWS` or `COPYING` files, one needs to use a `MANIFEST` file. issue_title: `activate.sh` cannot be sourced in a `setopt -e` script in zsh
issue_body:
if the zsh shell is using `-e`, aka `exit on any error`, then sourcing activate.sh doesn't work **any longer**. 
The problem is that the command `alias pydoc 2>/dev/null >/dev/null && unalias pydoc` fails, and causes the shell to exit (without any error message). Since not having `pydoc` alias when running the script is hardly  an erroneous condition, it shouldn't fail there.

Thus the `alias pydoc 2>/dev/null >/dev/null && unalias pydoc` should be changed to `alias pydoc 2>/dev/null >/dev/null && unalias pydoc || true`

issue_body: According to http://morepypy.blogspot.ru/2016/02/c-api-support-update.html lxml never really worked with pypy, but they should become compatible with pypy 4.1 and next lxml release. I think we should disable pypy tests for now.

[Docstring for `Selector` `.extract()` method](https://github.com/scrapy/parsel/blob/ae562c9a56450385cc50e8d28befcca13954b977/parsel/selector.py#L209) is wrong:

```
        """
        Serialize and return the matched nodes as a list of unicode strings.
        Percent encoded content is unquoted.
        """
```

It returns a single string, not a list of strings.

### Problem

The CSS3 spec allows the namespace field to be left empty, which indicates
an element with *no* namespace attached. However, cssselect cannot handle
those selectors right now. 

For example, suppose we have the following line:

```python
GenericTranslator().css_to_xpath('|foo')
```

This causes the parser to raise an exception:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/xpath.py", line 192, in css_to_xpath
    for selector in parse(css))
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 354, in parse
    return list(parse_selector_group(stream))
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 367, in parse_selector_group
    yield Selector(*parse_selector(stream))
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 375, in parse_selector
    result, pseudo_element = parse_simple_selector(stream)
  File "/cssselect/cssselect/parser.py", line 475, in parse_simple_selector
    "Expected selector, got %s" % (peek,))
cssselect.parser.SelectorSyntaxError: Expected selector, got <DELIM '|' at 0>
```

### Expected behaviour

cssselect should be able to handle a selector like `|foo`.

### Note

Here is the related part from [Selector Level 3 $6.1.1](https://www.w3.org/TR/selectors-3/#typenmsp):

```
ns|E
    elements with name E in namespace ns 
*|E
    elements with name E in any namespace, including those without a namespace 
|E
    elements with name E without a namespace 
E
    if no default namespace has been declared for selectors, this is equivalent to *|E.
    Otherwise it is equivalent to ns|E where ns is the default namespace. 
```

In case of one topic with one partition, if two balanced consumers are created within the same group for the topic, the later one would raise exception.  When the first balanced consumer is down, messages are not consumed anymore

However, in Java kafka client, the later kafka consumer would continue to consume the topic's messages.  The behavior is more resilient.

> 2016-04-18 06:25:09,420 ERROR           balancedconsumer        start:304       31791   Stopping consumer in response to error
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/service-venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py", line 299, in start
    self._add_self()
  File "/service-venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py", line 541, in _add_self
    raise KafkaException("Cannot add consumer: more consumers than partitions")

**PyKafka version**: 2.3.1
**Kafka version**: 0.8.2.1

According to the Kafka protocol documentation, `consumer_id` is invalid on group requests from non-managed consumers.

> Note that when this API is used for a "simple consumer," which is not part of a consumer group, then the generationId must be set to -1 and the memberId must be empty (not null). Additionally, if there is an active consumer group with the same groupId, then the commit will be rejected (typically with an UNKNOWN_MEMBER_ID or ILLEGAL_GENERATION error).

That means that [the consumer_id parameter](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/a9dd498392a2f78682719f44c56229cfff486c30/pykafka/simpleconsumer.py#L76) on `SimpleConsumer` should not actually be allowed. It should be deprecated in the next release and removed in the next major version.

The reason this parameter exists at all is because it's [used](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/a9dd498392a2f78682719f44c56229cfff486c30/pykafka/balancedconsumer.py#L411) by `ManagedBalancedConsumer`. The interface should be adjusted to allow `ManagedBalancedConsumer` to set this value appropriately for its needs without making it part of the public interface.

Andrew Boot from the [google group thread](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pykafka-user/2cBD-nWP-4Y) has offered to work on a pull request for this.

Hello!

Since the build was compiled using python2.7, it seems that it breaks the conditional dependencies here `https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/master/setup.py#L36`

So for instance, when installing stripe on python3, we now have a dependency on `simplejson`

One easy fix is to use https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0508/ to manage conditional dependencies.

issue_title: Debug builds don't work on Mac OS (i.e. with Clang)
issue_body:
**COPY-PASTING EMAIL**

On OS X, I'm having trouble building Debug builds, and I sort of figured out the issue, but someone else here might have more context.

I want to build a gdb-debuggable nupic.core. So I clear out my $NUPIC_CORE/build/scripts directory and run:
cmake $NUPIC_CORE -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=../release -DPY_EXTENSIONS_DIR=$NUPIC_CORE/bindings/py/nupic/bindings

Now
make -j3

Result:
[  9%] Executing Cap'n Proto compiler
Scanning dependencies of target nupic_core_solo
[  9%] Building CXX object src/CMakeFiles/nupic_core_solo.dir/nupic/proto/BitHistory.capnp.c++.o
error: invalid integral value 'g' in '-Og'
error: invalid integral value 'g' in '-Og'
make[2]: *** [src/CMakeFiles/nupic_core_solo.dir/nupic/proto/BitHistory.capnp.c++.o] Error 1

Doing some Googling, the issue is that it's building with Clang, which doesn't support '-Og', which was new to gcc 4.8.

I edited CMakeLists.txt, removing the occurrence of '-Og'. Clear out the scripts directory, rerun cmake, rerun make.

...and it works. I'm successfully stepping through code with gdb.

issue_body:
When trying to list all GSLBs in a zone, the 'get_all_gslb' function just repeats the parent zone name for N instances of GSLB under the zone.

To reproduce:
`my_zone = Zone('mydomain.com')
all_gslb = my_zone.get_all_gslb()
print(all_gslb)`

Will just print 
`[<GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com., <GSLB>: mydomain.com.]`

In dyn/zones.py, offending section removes the returned zone and fqdn:

`    def get_all_gslb(self):
        """Retrieve a list of all :class:`GSLB` services associated with this
        :class:`Zone`

        :return: A :class:`List` of :class:`GSLB` Services
        """
        uri = '/GSLB/{}/'.format(self._name)
        api_args = {'detail': 'Y'}
        response = DynectSession.get_session().execute(uri, 'GET', api_args)
        gslbs = []
        for gslb_svc in response['data']:

            gslbs.append(GSLB(self._name, self._fqdn, api=False, **gslb_svc))

issue_body:
Originally by *[hoangelos](https://github.com/hoangelos)* on 2011-11-22

To replicate the functionality of pages like http://arXiv.org/collection/astro-ph, we are recommending a description field to be added to the collections table.

When executing create-instance.sh, command
_echo '{}' | jinja2 $scriptpathname/instance.cfg > ../../var/${INVENIO_WEB_INSTANCE}-instance/${INVENIO_WEB_INSTANCE}.cfg_ will throw an exception like below about pyyaml.

`Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/bin/jinja2", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2cli/cli.py", line 335, in main
    sys.exit(cli(opts, args))
  File "/home/invenio/.virtualenvs/invenio3/lib/python2.7/site-packages/jinja2cli/cli.py", line 225, in cli
    raise InvalidDataFormat('%s: install pyyaml to fix' % format)
jinja2cli.cli.InvalidDataFormat: yaml: install pyyaml to fix`

In INSTALL.rst there is:

```bash
(invenio)$ pip install --process-dependency-links -e .[development]
```

Since it seems we are not using any more dependency links like we used to when this INSTALL file was created (https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio/blob/8e856b6edab2390e42c96db622c8ffa57907b547/setup.py#L55-L59) we can remove it from the installation instructions to not confuse the developers.

It is not recommended to use dependency links anyhow https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/2023

issue_title: api: schema validation fails on missing validation
issue_body:
Schema validation fails when you pass a URL in the ``$schema`` attribute:

**Works:**
```python
from invenio_records.api import Record
data = {
    "$schema": {
        "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/draft-04/schema#",
        "description": "A geographical coordinate",
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
            "latitude": { "type": "number" },
            "longitude": { "type": "number" }
        }
    },
    "latitude": 42,
    "longitude": 42,
}
Record.create(data)
```

**Doesn't work:**
```python
from invenio_records.api import Record
data = {
    "$schema": "http://json-schema.org/geo#",
    "latitude": 42,
    "longitude": 42,
}
Record.create(data)
```

Referring to the schema using JsonRef works, but that seems not to be compliant with the JSONSchema draft standard (http://json-schema.org/latest/json-schema-core.html#anchor22).

**Works:**
```python
from invenio_records.api import Record
data = {
    "$schema": {"$ref": "http://json-schema.org/geo#"},
    "latitude": 42,
    "longitude": 42,
}
Record.create(data)
```

issue_labels: [{'id': 414894568, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/labels/Need:%20information', 'name': 'Need: information', 'color': '719caa', 'default': False}, {'id': 414894566, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/labels/Size:%20hard', 'name': 'Size: hard', 'color': 'a828a8', 'default': False}, {'id': 411998258, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/labels/Status:%20in%20review', 'name': 'Status: in review', 'color': '3cbc3c', 'default': False}, {'id': 365704425, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/labels/Type:%20discussion', 'name': 'Type: discussion', 'color': 'ffaa00', 'default': False}, {'id': 365704422, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-records-files/labels/Type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'Type: enhancement', 'color': 'ff5500', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
**Problem:**
It is not intuitive that accessing `FilesMixin.files` will create a bucket if there is no bucket. A getter should not modify the database.

For B2Share deposits we want to create a bucket for every deposit. The bucket can later be deleted if the deposit is published without any file. The only way to do that with FilesMixin API is to call `deposit.files`. This looks more like a dead line of code than the creation of a bucket.

What's more, there is no way to know if a bucket exist with the API. One has to directly query the model. If, like I did, somebody tries to see if there are files in the serializer of invenio-records-rest, a bucket will be created automatically instead of saying that there are no files. This can create important bugs in case of rollback.

**Suggestion:**
- Add a method for creating the bucket. It could be a setter which takes a dict like this:

```
record.files = { 'hello.txt': BytesIO(b'Hello world!') }
```
- The `files` getter should not create a bucket if there is none. It can either return an iterator which ends immediately or return None. If an iterator is returned the bucket of the iterator should be `None`. That way it is explicit that there are no bucket.

issue_title: 4.4.1 Regression: `Components._utilities`/`_utility_registrations` can get out of sync with the cache
issue_body:
The `_UtilityRegistrations` object makes a reference to the `Component`'s `_utilities` and `_utility_registrations` when it is created. The `Component` object holds a strong reference to the `_UtilityRegistrations`. Presumably the `_UtilityRegistrations` holds these two references to avoid holding a reference to the `Component` instance and thus introducing a cycle.

The problem introduced in 4.4.1 is that if either of those two instance variables are assigned to, the cache object is not updated. Previously, accessing the cache would compare the identity of the objects it held with the identity of the objects the Component held and reset if they changed. Now that doesn't happen.

The use case is that subclasses may want to change the identity of `_utility_registrations` in the `registerUtility` method. Specifically, we have a subclass that will change the `_utility_registrations` object to a BTree instead of a dict when it grows to a certain size.

Now we *could* update our subclass to have a dependency on >= 4.4.1 and knowledge of the fact that the cache is now an instance attribute that also needs to be updated, but I'd prefer to see it handled here so that the cache is always consistent and guaranteed to be so.

issue_title: Numexpr evaluate function returns zero arrays when running on Xeon processors
issue_body:
Hello. I've recently discovered what appears to be a bug in Numexpr.

In a nutshell, `evaluate` sometimes (unpredictably) returns incorrect results when doing a straightforward array operation. The bug, which can be reproduced by the code below, results in a zero array being returned rather than the correct result. Although the sample code shows a multiplication, this bug has manifested for us on addition and exponentiation as well. Notably, there are no errors or warnings that are raised by Numexpr, the computational load appears normal (i.e. the RAM and CPU are taxed as expected when monitoring task manager), and the correct shape array is returned. It was a rather insidious bug to isolate for those reasons! In our tests, this bug has only manifested in the following hardware builds:
1. Windows Server 2012 r2, Intel Xeon 2680 v3,
   2 processors, 48 logical cores
2. Windows 8.1, Intel Xeon 2690,
   1 processor, 24 logical cores

In all the many thousands of runs of our software completed on our Windows 7, 64 bit, Intel i7 machines, this has never manifested. Furthermore, we have run the attached code many times (with bigger arrays and more iterations) and have not seen the error on the Windows 7, i7 machines. The Xeon computers, though, manifest it regularly. Unfortunately we don't have any other builds on which to test.

Any advice you have would be greatly appreciated. We have come to depend on Numexpr to do efficient multiprocessing on basic array operations in Python. The possibility of this error manifesting during a long run, though, without any kind of raised error or warning, is very problematic. 

Other items of note:
1. We are running from the WinPython distribution 3.4.3.6 and included Numexpr 2.4.4.
2. We have not invoked any supporting Numexpr functions, just `evaluate`... so we are using its default settings.

Thank you so much for writing such a useful tool, and thank you in advance for any help you can give us.

Sample Code:

```
import numpy as np
import numexpr as ne

x = np.ones(1e6)
y = np.ones(1e6)

for ii in range(1000):

    rr = ne.evaluate('x * y')
    test = np.all(rr == 0)
    if test:
        print('Gotcha! %d' % ii)

print('Complete!')
```
Just want to confirm that this library doesn't have GA support as the previous pandas.io did?

Right now the attenuator code has some unnecessary redundancy that makes the code hard to understand, like the empty class acting as a fake interface.
We should simplify the code and make it more straightforward to understand and use.

Hey,

I created the sub project libbrotli (https://github.com/bagder/libbrotli) a while ago to help build and install a *library* for brotli encoding/decoding, using only code from this repository. libbrotli is only a meta-project with mostly autotools to build, install and package a "library" for brotli since this original brotli home does not provide that. It only uses compression/decompression source code from the brotli tree.

This concept seems to resonate with a decent amount of users who appreciate being able to get a library (or two actually) out of a build and install process for use in various projects.

I would prefer if this functionality was provided by the brotli project itself and I'll offer to merge/translate it over to a pull-request or something if you'll agree this is interesting. I'd prefer to remove myself as a middle man here.

the problem happened when running the example program in readme.

OS: MacOS 10.13

issue_title: qgrid throws exception and locks up widget when sorting columns with mixed datatypes
issue_body:
When sorting a column of a DataFrame with mixed datatypes, the following exception is raised by pandas:

```
[IPKernelApp] ERROR | '>' not supported between instances of 'float' and 'str'
[IPKernelApp] ERROR | Unhandled exception while handling msg
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\qgrid\grid.py", line 743, in _handle_qgrid_msg
    self._handle_qgrid_msg_helper(content)
  File "C:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\qgrid\grid.py", line 805, in _handle_qgrid_msg_helper
    self._update_sort()
  File "C:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\qgrid\grid.py", line 491, in _update_sort
    inplace=True
  File "C:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\frame.py", line 3201, in sort_values
    na_position=na_position)
  File "C:\Anaconda3\lib\site-packages\pandas\core\sorting.py", line 247, in nargsort
    indexer = non_nan_idx[non_nans.argsort(kind=kind)]
TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'float' and 'str'
```

This is the expected behavior from pandas, but qgrid should prevent this from locking up the widget.

Here is a reproducible example:

```
import pandas as pd
import qgrid
df = pd.DataFrame({'A': [1.2, 'xy', 4]})
qgrid_widget = qgrid.QgridWidget(df=df, show_toolbar=True)
qgrid_widget
```

Clicking on the header of column 'A' or attempting to resize the column will raise the exception.

issue_title: Biased Mean Quantile Returns for Non-Equal Bins
issue_body:
I was doing a study today, and I came across a potential issue with the `performance.mean_return_by_quantile` logic in the returns tear sheet. I guess it is not so much an issue with the logic of that function, but more so how it is used in building the tear sheet. I think this would only be an issue if a user created bins or quantiles with a non-uniform number of observations in each bin over time. In other words, the amount of stocks in each bin changes over time.

If the number of stocks in a bin is correlated with market direction, this could bias the mean return calculation. For instance, if a factor quantile tends to have a lot of stocks when the market is going up, it will receive a higher weight on those up-days and will thus bias the mean return upwards for that factor quantiles. 

[Here is an example I created.](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1vVncTm_TKilb8bYjz5A2BdZ5pkacef-JgJrMApdEzig/edit?usp=sharingl) Note how it affects the mean return of the spread of going long the best quantile and short the worst quantile.

I believe the `mean_return_by_quantile` function already has the ability to group by date, so therefore, you could calculate the mean return for each quantile and date first, and then take the mean by quantile. 

Am I thinking about this right?

The Sortino Ratio is annualized in stats.py as follows:
`return sortino * ann_factor`

where `ann_factor` is
`ann_factor = annualization_factor(period, annualization)`

I believe you actually meant to return
`return sortino * np.sqrt(ann_factor)`

just like you did with the Sharpe Ratio.

I want to move all implementations to a model where the transport is explicit instead of using the less understood DSN magic. This is to standardize things across languages where sometimes the schema based model doesn't make a lot of sense.

i.e.

```
Raven(dsn='...', transport=ThreadedTransport)
```

This also means we dont have to load all these modules in-memory and it's less confusing to end-users on how to use your own.

issue_title: Jupyter notebook "hangs" when trying to display a Frame
issue_body: The issue stems from the console's "interactive" mode of display: when a table is shown in a console, it allows the user to scroll by listening to key presses. However in Jupyter notebook keypresses are not delivered to the terminal, and therefore the frame gets "stuck" (cannot exit interactive mode unless q is pressed).

I have a possibly interesting WCS problem.

I have a FITS header that is in TAN-SIP format, which should be readable by WCSTools, Starlink AST, and one other library that GalSim doesn't know how to talk to.  Given the warnings in fitswcs.py about WCSTools being slow, I went with Starlink AST.  The problem I'm having is that if I do something like
```
wcs = galsim.PyAstWCS('myfile.fits')
```
it makes a WCS with no problem and I can use the various methods like ```toImage()``` and so on.  But if I do
```
wcs = galsim.FitsWCS('myfile.fits')
```
then I get this warning "All the fits WCS types failed to read myfile.fits.  Using AffineTransform instead, which will not really be correct."  As far as I can tell, when the FitsWCS constructor is looping over the WCS types, it really is hitting an exception when it does this two-step process of reading in the header and then using the ```_readHeader``` method, unlike when directly instantiating the ```PyAstWCS``` directly.

In principle this isn't a very big deal; I could just require Starlink AST for these WCSs, instead of using FitsWCS (which would allow users with either Starlink AST or WCSTools to use these WCSs).  But it seemed a bit odd so I thought I would ask if this is something you've run into before, @rmjarvis , or have some ideas about?

I need to update to a new version of doxypy, since the old one is python2 only.

I'll do this here just to create a paper trail.

I'm not sure if there is much demand for this yet, but I was thinking about it recently, so I'm adding the issue so people can chime in here if they want us to pursue it.

If we do want to support python 3, I think we should go the route of trying to dual support both 2 and 3.  i.e. Have our code work no matter which version of python the user has.  A good description of how to do this (or at least a good starting point) is [here](http://python3porting.com/noconv.html).

To see how bad it might be, I ran 2to3 on the code, and the main thing it seems that we need to change is our print statements.  Some other things are the integer division, and apparently the import locations have rearranged a bit.  Also, basestr, which we check for at times is now str. But that link I gave above suggests ways to deal with all of these issues.  

There is one thing that seems to be a bit trickier, which is the syntax for metaclasses has changed.  But I'm sure StackOverflow will tell me how to deal with that.  :)

Anyway, I'll probably play around with this and see if I can get everything to work with both python varieties.  But it may not be terribly high priority for a while unless someone comments here that they want us to make it a high priority.

issue_title: Search-minus and search-plus have non-intuitive names
issue_body:
The lens, with a plus or a minus inside, is widely related to zoom in and zoom out, that is pricisely what the plus and minus imply. 
On the other hand, the relation between the lens and "search" is more coincidental, and usually not associated with the lens with the inner sign.

Thus I would suggest to change the name or create an alias for the lens to zoom-in or zoom-out, or an alias to "lens". I always have a very hard time to find the lens under the name of "search", I will usually try to find it by "lens", then "zoom", maybe "glass", "scale", and after a long while scanning through the icons, i finally find-it under "search".

Thanks

issue_title: Patroni should enforce the replication user password
issue_body:
At the moment, someone may change the password for the replication user on the master. This would result in a mismatch between the password written by Patroni to the pgpass in order to initialize the replica and the one that is actually in the database, breaking creation of new replicas. There is no way we can prevent the superuser from changing the password, so we cannot guarantee that already running master will keep the password, but at least we can set the password on an already existing cluster started by Patroni. This way, if someone runs Patroni on an already existing cluster that has a standby user with the password different from the one in the YAML file, they will be able to have replicas running normally.

The actual use case for this is initialization of the new Spilo cluster from the EBS snapshot corresponding to the old cluster. Since the password for the Spilo is normally set to a random value, and will not match the one stored in the database in the snapshot, the master initialized from the snapshot will use the old password and won't be able to talk to the replicas spawned by the autoscaling group, that will use the password from the spilo/patroni configuration (i.e. the random one).

issue_title: Intégration des indemnités journalières en cas d'accident de travail pour les AL
issue_body:
L'allocation logement se base sur les revenus N-2 ou, à défaut ou cas particulier, sur une évaluation forfaitaire du dernier mois.

Dans les calculs faits actuellement par OpenFisca, les indemnités journalières en cas d'arrêt maladie pour accident du travail ne sont pas pris en compte. Exemple : https://mes-aides.gouv.fr/foyer/resultat?situationId=5af9914e9ce308650d48ccd6 (cas extrême).

Plusieurs raisons à cela :
- le mode de calcul de l'évaluation forfaitaire : https://fr.openfisca.org/legislation/aide_logement_base_ressources_eval_forfaitaire
- la base ressources de l'allocation logement https://fr.openfisca.org/legislation/aide_logement_base_ressources_defaut
- (special MesAides) pour le N-2, il s'agit d'une donnée qui n'est pas capturée sur la page "Ressources n-2"

Pourtant elles devraient être prises en compte, d'après le site de la Caf :
> Le calcul de votre aide au logement tient compte de vos ressources et de celles de toutes les personnes vivant sous votre toit.
> Il s’agit des ressources telles que : les salaires, les pensions reçues, **les indemnités de chômage ou de maladie, d’accident de travail**, etc.
> Les allocations familiales, le Rsa, l’Aah ou la Prime d'activité ne sont pas pris en compte dans le calcul de vos ressources.

C'est renforcé par le fait que sur la simulation Caf sur caf.fr, lors de la déclaration N-2, en plus des infos de la feuille d'impôt, le montant des IJ est demandé. Et avec comme c'est le cas dans la simulation extrème 45K d'IJ... c'est mort pour l'AL.

Quelqu'un peut m'éclairer sur le comportement d'OpenFisca vis à vis des IJ d'accident du travail et de l'allocation logement ? Merci !

(sujet remonté suite à un retour utilisateur)

issue_title: Fix whitespace handling in filenames where "list:data:fastq:" objects are inputs
Add support for configuring/viewing API Gateway logs
issue_body:
While chalice can support retrieving logs from lambda via the `chalice logs` command, it's not possible to retrieve logs for API gateway.  Additionally, creating a lambda function automatically creates a log group for you so there's nothing you need to do to configure logs.

It would be helpful to also have this same functionality for API gateway.  This needs to be explicitly configured when creating your rest API.  The other issue I remember running into is that you set a role that API gateway uses to push logs to cloudwatch, but this role is for your entire account.  It's not clear to what extent chalice should set this up for you.  You need a role configured or else you don't get APIG logs, but it may already be configured.  We probably will need to add a few config options for the various use cases.

Originally requested in https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/841#issuecomment-389747973

issue_title: FDK produces broken font in Windows when AGL and GOADB conflicts
issue_body:
If one assigns a unicode value to a glyph when there is another glyph in the font gets that unicode same value from the AGL, the resulting font will install and work in OS X, but Windows will refuse to install the font.

Round-tripping the font through fontTools will fix the font to be installable in Windows.

It appears, via FontValidator, that the cmap produced has the endCode array not in ascending order, at least that's the error that is given by FontValidator.

I don't know if this is actually a bug or not — but if the FDK encounters this, at least a warning should be thrown, if not a refusal to compile the font if this behavior isn't considered a bug.

issue_title: Sharding with range filters results in poor workload distribution
issue_body:
This is a somewhat old issue that I doesn't seem to have gotten much attention. I found a Google Groups post that articulates the problem and proposes a fix for it here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/app-engine-pipeline-api/O_5aaoNsS04

Problems summary:
Presently equality[sic] filters are ignored when choosing key range split points using the __scatter__ property. The resulting split points are appropriate for the entire population of entities of a given kind, but not necessarily for the sub-population matching the equality filter(s). This can result in a very uneven distribution of work among shards.

I thought I'd re-file it here in case that group is no longer monitored.
The fix seems simple enough and I'm happy to create a local branch with it, but the performance impact is huge enough that it would seem prudent to get it into the main branch.

issue_title: BigQuery: doesn't declare dependency on concurrent.futures

I'm looking to replace glyphicons by another font, and the solution is quite hard as it will then be boring for the future updates.

What I suggest is to move glyphicons in a gem and update the `README.md` in order to suggest to add that gem.
This would allow anyone to use another gem adding another font.

For instance, let say someone want to use the standard stack, he would have in his `Gemfile`

```
source 'https://rubygems.org'

...
gem 'bootstrap-sass'
gem 'font-glyphicons-sass'
gem 'sass-rails'
...
```

while another guy would like to use another font

```
source 'https://rubygems.org'

...
gem 'bootstrap-sass'
gem 'font-awesome-sass'
gem 'sass-rails'
...
```

This solution would avoid any difficulties to upgrade Bootstrap. Another advantage is that when an update of the font is done, only the glyphicons gem would be updated limiting the download size, and vise-versa.

I've recently noticed that git globally ignored filed are included in a npm package when publishing it and as a result some files are published by accident. See https://github.com/npm/npm/issues/5673 (and I'm working on https://github.com/rom1504/npm-safeguard)

The last version published of this package contain files in a .idea/. It doesn't seem like they contain actually private information in this case, but publishing these files by accident can lead to publishing passwords or any other private information (that would be put in webstorm run configurations for example)

issue_title: Dropdown Menus stopped working after adding Fullcalendar
issue_body:
I have a simple Rails application that I am trying to add Fullcalendar to.  After modifying my application.js file with "//= require moment" and "//= require fullcalendar" my dropdown menus stop working.  When you clicked on them nothing at all happened except for a "#" sign was being added to the end of the URL in the browser's address bar.  After a few days of looking for a solution I found information about the order of things in the application.css and the applicatoin.js files.  It turns out that the above two mentioned requires need to be after "//= require jquery" and "//= require jquery_ujs" lines in the application.js file.  I have no idea why, but I sure other people well run into the same problem.

I hope that this information helps.

The following command results in the failure I'm running into on my Mac (running 10.12.6):

➜  pex -v --disable-cache --python=python3 doit --platform macosx_10.12-x86_64 --platform linux-x86_64 -o doit.pex

    pex_builder = build_pex(reqs, options, resolver_options_builder)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pex/bin/pex.py", line 601, in build_pex
    for dist in resolveds:
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pex/resolver.py", line 438, in resolve_multi
    allow_prereleases):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pex/resolver.py", line 376, in resolve
    return resolver.resolve(resolvables_from_iterable(requirements, builder))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pex/resolver.py", line 209, in resolve
    dist = self.build(package, resolvable.options)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pex/resolver.py", line 177, in build
    raise Untranslateable('Package %s is not translateable by %s' % (package, translator))
pex.resolver.Untranslateable: Package SourcePackage('https://pypi.python.org/packages/28/2e/1ff399cfd2a6a8ebb65152203c920643c2169aa507b2e96559d19baeb7c3/MacFSEvents-0.8.1.tar.gz#md5=45759553d58bab6f7c8d6187fb0fe12f') is not translateable by ChainedTranslator(WheelTranslator, EggTranslator, SourceTranslator)

It appears as if `pex` is succeeding in building the part of the PEX for `macosx_10.12-x86_64` (makes sense since that's my host OS), but failing on the part for `linux-x86_64`.

@@ -12,6 +14,6 @@ package org.pantsbuild.example.hello.simple;

   public static void main(String[] args) {
-    System.out.println("Hello World!");
+    System2.out.println("Hello World!");
   }
 }
```

After upgrading to org.pantsbuild zinc version 1.0.5 the output from the javac compiler is not complete.

                         [warn] sun.security.x509.X500Name

I enabled the `-fork-java` option in pants.ini and this seems to fix the output truncation.

index 8e76da7..e264cf0 100644
--- a/pants.ini
+++ b/pants.ini

issue_title: RemotePantsRunner initializes a scheduler before connecting
issue_body:
Currently, RemotePantsRunner calls [self.pantsd.maybe_launch()](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/bin/remote_pants_runner.py#L115), which calls `PantsDaemon.Factory.create`, which calls `PantsDaemon.Factory._setup_legacy_graph_helper`.

This results in a lot more initialization than is actually necessary to either launch or connect to the daemon. In my profiling, almost a second is spent in `_setup_legacy_graph_helper` (a problem in itself! but avoidable here).

issue_title: `python_dist` doesn't safeguard against pex by-version caching, may provide stale dists when used
issue_body:
As of #5141, Pants has a new target that allows users to define setup.py-based distributions. A new Pants task will run the user-provided setup.py and produce a wheel for consumption by other Python targets.

Currently, the pex resolver handles resolving these dists into the output pex, and caches them using the name of the wheel in the pex resolver cache. This can be problematic if two `python_dist` targets in the same project have the same name and version info in their respective setup.py's, leading to undefined behavior in the event that there is a collision in the pex resolver cache. 

One solution is to write a `setuptools` plugin to hijack setup.py execution and inject a local version identifier into the wheel name within the context of Pants task execution, allowing the resolver to safely cache wheels because wheel names will contain their corresponding `python_dist` target's unique sha.

cc @kwlzn

When `pantsd` is in use, `BUILD` file parse errors in contexts involving multiple `BUILD` files experience a very reproducible race that the thread attempting to call `with_fork_context` (from #5521) to hang while trying to shut down the runtime.


Thread backtraces [arc attached](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/files/2338306/pantsd-error-hang.txt) that show thread 10 attempting to drop the runtime while forking, while runtime threads threads 2, 3 and 4 are attempting to use the runtime to launch more work.

-----

Post #6013, when a root Node in the graph fails, we fail both `fast` and `slow` to some degree:
* We fail `fast` in that the root Node for a request will fail immediately as soon as any of its dependencies have failed, and `scheduler.product_request` will return quickly with that failure.
* But we also fail `slow`ly, in that any work that a failing Node depended on it that _didn't_ fail itself will continue to run to completion.

The race occurs when work is still running (due to the `slow` case) when a `fast` failure has occurred, and `pantsd` is preparing to fork.

issue_title: Embedding model verification data into PMML documents
issue_body:
It should be possible to initialize the `<Model>/ModelVerification` element during conversion:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/47229306/any-way-to-auto-generate-pmml-model-verification-section-from-rows-in-dataframe

One way would be to create a meta-estimator that wraps the original estimator and the associated estimator verification data:
```Python
pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
  ..,
  VerifiedEstimator(LogisticRegression(), verification_data = ...)
])
```

Another way would be to add the verification data straight to the `sklearn2pmml()` function:
```Python
sklearn2pmml(pipeline, "pipeline.pmml", verification_data = ...)
```

issue_title: Some sample learners from generate-user-data appearing out of order
issue_body: On Coach Learner Reports, these two appear at top of list Link to beta page: http://kolibribeta.learningequality.org/coach/#/fa4cbfeda32c0c0fbf1832fc1ddd10c3/learners Observations: 1. "Jikutokuruka" and "Takitoteka" don't appear in alphabetical order (rest of list is alphabetized)

## Summary

I think committing to the "tree structure" [data model](http://kolibri.readthedocs.io/en/latest/dev/content/concepts_and_definitions.html) will lead to significant limitation for content reuse. The problem is discussed below, and an alternative is proposed.


## Scenario
Consider the following use case:

  - author creates content node `quadratic equation` which is a summary of how to use the quadratic equation, that consists of three subnodes:
    ```
    quadratic_eqns = ContentNode(title='Quadratic equations')
    intro = ContentNode(title='Intro paragraph', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    formulas = ContentNode(title='Quadratic formula', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    proof = ContentNode(title='Derivation of the q. eqn', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    extra = ContentNode(title='Extra explanations', parent_id=quadratic_eqns.id)
    ```
  - author creates two tutorials that include the `quadratic_eqns`
     - Basic math techniques
       - math topic 1
       - math topic 2
       - solving quadratic equations
       - math topic n
     - Solving kinematics problems
       - phys topic 1
       - phys topic 2
       - solving quadratic equations
       - phys topic n

Using the current data model (where each `ContentNode` has a unique `parent_id`) it would be impossible for the ContentNode `quadratic_eqns` to be used in both places.

Furthermore, if the current data model is somehow modified to allow `ContentNode`s  to be reused in multiple locations another problem would arise if the author chooses to customize the nodes that will be included as part of the two tutorials.

Assume the author wants to include different subsets of the `ContentNode`s from the `quadratic_eqns`. The author wants the first tutorial (Basic math techniques) to include the full contents: `[intro,formulas,proof,extra]`, whereas the second tutorial (Solving kinematics problems) should include just the summary `[intro,formulas]`.

The author will have to "fork" `quadratic_eqns` ContentNode and make two versions, one with the full contents and one with the reduced contents.

## Unnecessary coupling of concerns
It seems there is unnecessary coupling between the tree-like structure needed for presentation:

   - `parent_id`
   - `sort_order`
   - `lft`
   - `rght`
   - `tree_id` (not sure what this does)
   - `level`

And the content/metadata needs:

  - `license_id`/`license_owner`
  - `title`
  - `content_id`
  - `description`
  - `author`
  - `kind`
  - `available`
  - `stemmed_metaphone` ??
  - `has_prerequisite`
  - `related`
  - `tags`


## Alternative
Like with all things in software architecture, one possible solution would be to add a level of indirection between `ContentNode` objects and the actual contents.

For example a new model `ContentItem` could be build that focuses on the content/metadata concerns and each `ContentNode` can "point to" a `ContentItem`. This will allow for reuse of `ContentItem`s in multiple contexts.

A useful analogy for thinking about this proposed change is `ContentItems` as songs, and the (hierarchy of) `ContentNode`s as a playlist of pointers to songs.

I have been thinking about "data representation" for educational content for the past 10+ years so I'll be happy to discuss further.

## How to reproduce

According to Design Document:

issue_title: pip config relies on pywb master branch and not dev
issue_body: `setup.py` needs to be changed to reference the dev branch of the pywb package but pypi does not like a URI being specified there and expects a package name. Same goes for the surt dependency. The correct branch/version IS specified in requirements.txt.

ipwb currently explicitly requires the surt module in the requirements.txt file. pywb, another dependency of ipwb, also includes surt. Remove the explicit surt dependency from ipwb and reference pywb's version. Per discussion in #36 w/ @ikreymer and @ibnesayeed .

issue_title: Archived non-absolute HTTP redirects are not replayed correctly
issue_body:
According to [RFC7231](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-7.1.2), the HTTP location response header may be a URI fragment, e.g., Location: `/foo.html`.

When replaying a redirect from http://localhost:5000/memento/20180727203127/example.com/ to http://localhost:5000/memento/20180727203127/example.com/anotherURI as in https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/blob/master/ipwb/samples/warcs/redirectRelative.warc, the user is instead redirected to http://localhost:5000/memento/20180727203127//anotherURI.

This has to do with the logic of URI resolution to the replay system. Ideally, the ServiceWorker would handle this but it does not appear to do so.

issue_title: 'ignore' only ignores files from next layer, not any base layers
issue_body:
## Checklist

 - [X] Confirmed this is an issue with charm-tools, not charmstore-client
 - [X] Provide versions of tools used
 - [X] Described the feature or ways to replicate the issue

## What version am I running?

I ran the following command: `charm version` and got the following ouput:

```
charm 2.2.0-0ubuntu1~ubuntu16.04.1~ppa2
charm-tools 2.2.0
```

I am using: Ubuntu 16.04 LTS

## Issue

The `ignore` tactic is documented as follows in the docstring of `IgnoreTactic`:

    If a given layer's ``layer.yaml`` has an ``ignore`` list, then any file
    or directory included in that list that is provided by base layers will
    be ignored, though any matching file or directory provided by the current
    or any higher level layers will be included.

However, this is not what I observe in practice: it in fact only ignores files provided by the immediate next layer.  Here's a reduced test case:

    name: charm-test-1
    summary: Charm test 1

    series:

issue_title: Add a default for --outputDir to bin/noninteractive-alignment-panel.py or make it required
issue_body: `bin/noninteractive-alignment-panel.py` does not have a default for `--outputDir` and the argument is not specified as `required=True`. So leaving it out results in an exception. issue_title: Windows: SyntaxError: (unicode error) 'unicodeescape' codec can't decode bytes in position 3-4: truncated \UXXXXXXXX escape
issue_body:
Got a message from someone not able to run local windows. The error was flagged in there sascfg.py on their classpath line. I've reproduced the error (which I hadn't seen before) and am documenting it here. I'll add it to the Troubleshooting doc next. 
The problem is having a \u  or \U (either way) in a string - like in the classpath in the sascfg.py file:

cpW += ";C:\Users\user\AppData\Roaming\Python\Python36\site-packages\saspy\java\saspyiom.jar"

The error is like in the title of this issue. The fix is to either code \\u or \\U  anywhere you happen to have \u in you strings, or prefix the string with an 'r' (for raw string - don't transcode) as follows (either of these will work):

cpW += ";C:\\Users\user\AppData\Roaming\Python\Python36\site-packages\saspy\java\saspyiom.jar"
cpW += r";C:\Users\user\AppData\Roaming\Python\Python36\site-packages\saspy\java\saspyiom.jar"

issue_title: Fixed in #605 - Client.inspect_image("") behaves like Client.images()
issue_body:
```py
>>> from os import environ
>>> from docker import Client
>>> c = Client(environ['DOCKER_HOST'])
>>> type(c.images())
<type 'list'>
>>> >>> len(c.images()) > 0
True
>>> c.inspect_image('') == c.images()
True
```

This isn't hard to see why. `inspect_image()` calls `/images/{image_id}/json`. `images()` calls `/images/json`. Where the `{image_id}` is an empty string, the [resulting URL](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/bf00acf854a3b78335c1157f4a1a83488e611bb8/docker/client.py#L751) for the call to `inspect_image()` is `/images//json`:

```sh
% [ "$( curl --location --post30{1,2,3} --silent 'http://<host>:2375/images//json' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'Accept: application/json' -X GET )" \
> = "$( curl --location --post30{1,2,3} --silent 'http://<host>:2375/images/json' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'Accept: application/json' -X GET )" ] \
> && echo 'SAME' || echo 'NOT THE SAME'
SAME
```

By analogy, I suspect the `inspect_container()` and `containers()` methods suffer from a similar problem.

The expected format of the `inspect_*()` methods (a detailed object) is different from the corresponding `*s()` methods (an array of summary objects), so it seems to me calling `inspect_*("")` should probably raise an exception. Maybe something like:

```py
    @check_resource
    def inspect_container(self, container):
        ...
        if not container:
            raise ValueError('container ID required')
        ...

    @check_resource
    def inspect_image(self, image):
        ...
        if not image:
            raise ValueError('image ID required')
        ...
```

I can create a PR if desired.

For example, I might keep my raspberry pi on to download videos, but wouldn't want it to consume the entire internet bandwidth. wget has a _--limit-rate <rate>_ option, so it could be passed on from the top level if wget is selected as the downloader by the user.

issue_title: check sorting of concatenated elements found in _endElements
issue_body: We need to store an additional flag in the location object to say that an object is an _endElement.  Here's why: say we have two streams, the first contains a lot of notes and then in _endElements we put a keySignature and then a repeatBarline.  Stream 2 begins with a clef change and then notes.  If we create a new stream (S0) that contains S1 + S2 and then do S0.flat, the autosort will put the clef before the keySignature change (and probably also before the repeat sign).  So I'd like it that elements in the _endElements list sort before elements in the following stream.  I don't think it'd be too hard.  One simple way is to add a huge negative number to the sort priority for all _endElements.
It appears that #342 is still broken... using the walkthrough code, a VERY long legend is generated when making a trajectory plot and drift plot. Passing "legend = False" turns off the legend.

With #138 fixed, the operator `raw` now has 2 roles:

1. Input contents that will otherwise create a conflict in DSC interface.
2. Input a parameter whose value depends on another one. For example:

```
n: 3
m: raw(n * 2)
```

will make sure that `m` is evaluated after other parameters that do not have `raw` in them.

So I think a more appropriate name for `raw` is perhaps `delayed`? Any other suggestions?

issue_title: Input values are not validated before patch operation
issue_body:
Body is not validated before patch operation

For example: BIOS property patching
Actual: When user is trying to patching the property or value which is improper, the API doesn’t through any exception.

Expected: API should prompt proper exception.

Before patch operation validation of the body(property and value) is necessary and the all supported properties/values/field can be found http://h22208.www2.hpe.com/eginfolib/servers/docs/HPRestfultool/iLo4/data_model_reference.html

settings = { "PowerProfile": "MaxPerf"}
response = bios_rest_obj.patch(path = uri,
                                          body = settings,
                                          optionalpassword = None)

For property PowerProfile:
String containing one of the following supported values:
"BalancedPowerPerf" Balanced Power and Performance
"MinPower"  Minimum Power Usage
"MaxPerf"   Maximum Performance
"Custom"    Custom

If user tries to patch any value other than the supported values then exception should be thrown.
Even if user tries with Property name being wrong then also it shouldn’t be supported(Example: settings = { "Invalid_Property_Name": "Invalid_Value"}

issue_title: PacerRssFeeds() shifts times by the court UTC offset, changing dates
issue_body: This is a narrow sub-issue of #223, but `feedparser`'s `published_parsed` date is a UTC tuple. Take this example of Paul Manafort's document from yesterday: ```>>> pprint(f.entries[34]) {'guidislink': False, 'id': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/DktRpt.pl?190597&1345', 'link': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/DktRpt.pl?190597', 'links': [{'href': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/DktRpt.pl?190597', 'rel': u'alternate', 'type': u'text/html'}], 'published': u'Tue, 05 Jun 2018 00:20:36 GMT', 'published_parsed': time.struct_time(tm_year=2018, tm_mon=6, tm_mday=5, tm_hour=0, tm_min=20, tm_sec=36, tm_wday=1, tm_yday=156, tm_isdst=0), 'summary': u'[Revoke] (315)', 'summary_detail': {'base': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/rss_outside.pl', 'language': None, 'type': u'text/html', 'value': u'[Revoke] (315)'}, 'title': u'1:17-cr-00201-1 USA v. MANAFORT et al', 'title_detail': {'base': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/rss_outside.pl', 'language': None, 'type': u'text/plain', 'value': u'1:17-cr-00201-1 USA v. MANAFORT et al'}} ``` It was filed at `Mon Jun 4 20:20:36 EDT 2018` but the RSS feed is expressed in UTC: `Tue, 05 Jun 2018 00:20:36 GMT`, and so therefore the date is a UTC date. But we treat it as a local date and cut off the time: https://github.com/freelawproject/juriscraper/blob/97fc853db3b8971af3e24f3b0562ab3346faf7fa/juriscraper/pacer/rss_feeds.py#L201 which means: ``` >>> f.entries[34].published_parsed[:3] (2018, 6, 5) ``` But of course the proper date is June 4, not June 5. Ironically this fails whether CL is storing the DateTime in court-local-time (US/Eastern, here) or in Mike-local-time (US/Pacific), and I have honestly lost track of which it really is (but that's OK, I bet the CourtListener code has also lost track `:(`), but a fix to #223 will fix this too, "for free." The result is the CL docket had the wrong date for this document (June 5) until someone ran the PACER Docket Report in RECAP and then it flipped over to June 4 (this says something about data models...). You can still find the same problem at https://www.courtlistener.com/docket/6174582/vasquez-v-county-of-will-illinois/?filed_after=&filed_before=&entry_gte=&entry_lte=&order_by=desc: Where of course the document was filed on June 4 ```js {'guidislink': False, 'id': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/DktRpt.pl?190469&125', 'link': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/DktRpt.pl?190469', 'links': [{'href': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/DktRpt.pl?190469', 'rel': u'alternate', 'type': u'text/html'}], 'published': u'Tue, 05 Jun 2018 03:14:50 GMT', 'published_parsed': time.struct_time(tm_year=2018, tm_mon=6, tm_mday=5, tm_hour=3, tm_min=14, tm_sec=50, tm_wday=1, tm_yday=156, tm_isdst=0), 'summary': u'[Memorandum in opposition to motion] (34)', 'summary_detail': {'base': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/rss_outside.pl', 'language': None, 'type': u'text/html', 'value': u'[Memorandum in opposition to motion] (34)'}, 'title': u'1:17-cv-02194 VASQUEZ v. COUNTY OF WILL, ILLINOIS, et. al', 'title_detail': {'base': u'https://ecf.dcd.uscourts.gov/cgi-bin/rss_outside.pl', 'language': None, 'type': u'text/plain', 'value': u'1:17-cv-02194 VASQUEZ v. COUNTY OF WILL, ILLINOIS, et. al'}} ``` ``` pb3:search jhawk$ gdate -d 'Tue, 05 Jun 2018 03:14:50 GMT' Mon Jun 4 23:14:50 EDT 2018 ```

EIA Electric System Operating Data has an API for the hourly load (actual and forecast), generation, and imports/exports from all balancing authorities in the lower 48 states! This is an amazing complement to the data provided from the ISOs themselves.

To implement, create a file `pyiso/eia_esod.py` and add a new client class with the standard methods `get_generation`, `get_load`, and `get_trade`. Each method should take a balancing authority abbreviation as an argument, similar to how some `get_lmp` methods take a node name. All returned data should be in the `RTHR` and `DAHR` markets, and generation should be fuel `other`. 

The query browser will be useful for writing the parsers, eg http://www.eia.gov/opendata/qb.cfm?category=2122628&sdid=EBA.CISO-ALL.D.H

Target: CI environment & scripts
Not for daily developer's use

issue_body:
Sorry for asking questions here, not really sure where to. I try asking on IRC sometimes but oftentimes no one is available to answer.

Can I still run Deis in the "stateless" mode on Deis v2 (kubernetes-based)? I understand it doesn't have Ceph anymore, but it seems a lot of problems arise in my AWS cluster that can possibly be alleviated by statelessness.

EC2 regularly changes IPs of machines which causes problems intermittently... I'd like to avoid these headaches when they happen.

issue_title: Design Doc: Support Private Registries Behind Auth
issue_body:
Goal: 
To support enterprise stacks without the need for `git push deis` which is often slow in most normal network conditions.

Code Changes:
Client - implement flag -f --forward-credentials
  - this can utilize a users docker login, to forward credentials from ~/.docker/auth.json
  - this would be familiar to users of other schedulers
Builder - go-dockerclient and docker remote api support the x-registry-auth and related headers.
Our builder should leverage theses with the forwarded credentials

By always forwarding credentials we improve security dramatically by not storing state in the cluster.

Testing:
  Unit tests should be implemented at the builder level for param validation.
  Functional tests should test the relationship between the client and the builder service.


Any suggestions?

issue_title: Proposal: controller should republish certain things to etcd at startup
issue_body:
Supersedes #3486 and #3539

In the event a new cluster is built as a replacement for an old one and the database is either external (as in the case of running Cephless) or is restored from a backup of the old cluster, certain necessary configuration that _is_ present in the database needs to be republished to etcd.

The controller could do this at startup.

I would propose (at least) the following things be republished:

* Application configuration
* Application domains
* User keys

Relates to #4333

My understanding is that the following is all that is required to cause requests, traces (exceptions) and custom events (logs) to be sent to Application Insights:
applicationinsights.flask.ext.AppInsights(flask.Flask("foo"))

I'm not completely clear on whether I need to be manually calling .flush() or not - please advise.

Either way, I've tried:
app = flask.Flask("foo")
app_insights = applicationinsights.flask.ext.AppInsights(app)
app.logger.info("test")
app_insights.flush()

Requests are logged but no custom events appear in app insights. No errors.

issue_body:
from tsfresh import feature_extraction

When executing the above code, I got the following warning:

/Users/junhaoxiong/anaconda3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/statsmodels/compat/pandas.py:56: FutureWarning: The pandas.core.datetools module is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. Please use the pandas.tseries module instead.
  from pandas.core import datetools

I have just install tsfresh. The pandas version I am using is 0.20.3. 

Any help would be appreciated!

When trying to run the [tsfresh/notebooks/timeseries_forecasting_google_stock.ipynb](https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/blob/master/notebooks/timeseries_forecasting_google_stock.ipynb) Jupyter Notebook I ran into a few issues. I'm currently working through ironing them out and will put up a pull request when done. These were all encountered with `tsfresh==0.11.0` in Python 3.6.5

1. Pandas datareader: the `google` information source no longer seems to work. This can be replaced by `iex` (and then adjusting the column names).
2. When predicting with adaboost, the prediction observation first needs to be reshaped to two-dimensional. 
3. Plotting in matplotlib or pandas has changed so the x-axis labels were messy.
4. Some of the comments regarding the plots and feature importances were out of order or off (probably because of different results when running the notebook multiple times).

These don't affect the content of the notebook, but they do prevent it from being run!

Can I just confirm that in terms of the DataFrame passed to extract_features, the
DataFrame columns must be named with a string or at least > 0 digit?

The document indeed demonstrates named columns in all the DataFrame examples,
however it does not specifically say that the DataFrame cannot be default
0-indexed and perhaps there is a good reason for that.

This is easily worked around by naming the columns as per all the DataFrame examples and resolves this e.g.:

```
df.columns = ['metric', 'timestamp', 'value']
df_features = extract_features(df, column_id='metric', column_sort='timestamp', column_kind=None, column_value=None)
```
But just for clarification, I am constructing a single timeseries Flat DataFrame with just the default
pandas df indexing

```
                                0           1         2
0     stats.statsd.bad_lines_seen  1478736060  0.000000
1     stats.statsd.bad_lines_seen  1478736120  0.000000
2     stats.statsd.bad_lines_seen  1478736180  0.000000
```

And then passing extract_features the following parameters:

```python
df_features = extract_features(df, column_id=0, column_sort=1, column_kind=None, column_value=None)
```

This causes the following error:

```

And having poked around it is not that simple as utilities/dataframe_functions.py

```python
>>> column_id = 0
>>> if column_id:

```

And even if that is changed in utilities/dataframe_functions.py to handle
passing 0 e.g.

```python
#    if column_id:
    if column_id or column_id == 0:
```

That just raises 

```
--> 259                 raise AttributeError("The given column for the id is not present in the data.")
    260             elif kind_to_df_map[kind][column_id].isnull().any():
    261                 raise ValueError("You have NaN values in your id column.")

AttributeError: The given column for the id is not present in the data.
```

So without having to reverse engineer all the kind_to_df_map stuff, it is easier
for the timebeing to just note in the documentation that the DataFrame columns
should not be 0-indexed?

Or maybe it is just an issue that has not been reported?

issue_title: Move fork and chain specific constants into their individual modules
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The `evm.constants` module has a number of values that are *fork specific* such as `FRONTIER_DIFFICULTY_ADJUSTMENT`.  Having these in the `evm.constants` module feels *wrong* since the core Py-EVM library is supposed to be largely agnostic about specific fork rules.

### How can it be fixed

These variables would be more appropriately located if they were located under the fork module that they apply to.  Move each of the fork specific and chain specific constants into a new module at  `evm.vm.forks.<fork-name>.constants` or `evm.chains.mainnet.constants`.

It would be great if the `conda` tool gained support for `environment.lock.yml` files. These files should be the auto-generated equivalent to the `environment.yml` file, just like the `Gemfile.lock` file is the auto-generated equivalent to the `Gemfile` in Ruby-land.

The `environment.lock.yml` file would fix version numbers of all packages used in this project. I believe that the output of `conda env export` is mostly sufficient for this, except that the `prefix:` entry should be omitted.

I would propose to [copy the behaviour from bundler](http://bundler.io/v1.3/man/bundle-install.1.html#DESCRIPTION) regarding what to do if a lock file exists or doesn't, and what to do if the lock file and the regular environment file are out of sync. I also believe that bundler's [conservative updating](http://bundler.io/v1.3/man/bundle-install.1.html#CONSERVATIVE-UPDATING) is an approach that `conda` should mimic.

I propose that this functionality is incorporated into `conda env create`. This means that you can re-run `conda env create` even when an environment with the same name already exists. This would be a departure of current behaviour - currently, `conda env create` aborts with an error if an environment with the same name already exists.

I would further propose that `conda env update` updates all dependencies based on `environment.yml` (like it currently does), and also updates the `environment.lock.yml` accordingly, just like `bundle update`. This should not introduce any breaking changes.

The discussion around [Pipfile](https://github.com/pypa/pipfile) shows that this problem is still a concern in Python land. It would be great if Anaconda gained such functionality.

issue_title: documented way to detect which conda environment is active, read conda-meta .json
issue_body:
I have been transitioning a build system to work against conda. A feature of the build system is building the link line compiling C++ applications. It would be nice if I could detect if a conda environment is active from my scripts, as well as which one, and then further go into the conda-meta directory and parse the .json files there to read what shared object libraries are associated with different packages - I realize that guessing which of the .so files listed in the .json files is needed to link against the package is not bullet proof, the goal is doing something that gets it right most of the time.

I had been looking at the environment variable (this is in linux) CONDA_ENV_PATH in a previous version of conda, but now I see I need to look at CONDA_PREFIX. Since it has changed, I realize my method of determining which conda environment is active is hacky.

Is it possible to nail down a environment variable that users could rely on to detect which conda environment is active? Can I rely on the subdirectory named 'conda-meta' being present, along with the current structure of the .json files in there?

issue_title: conda clean -p breaks hard linking of new installs
issue_body:
@asmeurer 
Following on from conda/conda#68:

On Windows 8 (and probably equally on all other windows versions), `conda clean -p` removes all extracted packaged from the `pkgs` directory even if they are hard linked in some environments.

While all existing environments will continue to function, creation of a new environment with the same packages causes conda to extract the packages again and  to hard link to the newly extracted files. This massively increases disk usage and results in the opposite of what a user running `conda clean` was attempting to achieve.

The problem lies in `main_clean.py` and the fact that on Windows  `lstat(file).st_nlink` always returns 0, even if `file` is hard linked. (This seems to have been fixed from python 3.2 onwards: https://bugs.python.org/issue10027)

As a stop gap measure `conda clean -p` should be prevented from being run on windows until a better solution can be found.

```

issue_title: activate.d scripts is likely not ran in order.
issue_body:
scripts in  /etc/conda/activate.d are likely not ran in order:

```
   if [[ -d "$_CONDA_D" ]]; then
        eval $(find "$_CONDA_D" -iname "*.sh" -exec echo source \'{}\'';' \;)
    fi

```

See here:
https://serverfault.com/questions/181787/find-command-default-sorting-order

If there are interdependency between scripts this will be very badly messed up. -- I am looking into if we can make the new cross-compilation enabled mpich package to fall back to use CXX and CC when the cross-toolchain is not installed (the result should still work on the local system since it is ABI compatable). For this to work, however, it depends on the cross compilation activate.d scripts to run before the mpich one.

issue_title: Solver improvements: 5) explore ways to invoke the SAT solver more incrementally
issue_body:
ref: https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415503020

@mcg1969 in https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415226906
> One challenge we face here is that while our problems are somewhat incremental, they do involve some backtracking. From a mathematical point of view, our model is actually a multi-objective, pseudoboolean optimization problem. We start with a set of mandatory logical clauses that must be satisfied; then we proceed by performing a sequence of pseudoboolean optimization passes, implemented with bisection. We handle the conversion of the pseudoboolean inequalities to standard logical form using a BDD approach.
> 
> So at first glance we could exploit an incremental approach, but many of our bisection steps are unsatisfiable. When that happens, we have to *roll back* the latest set of incremental clauses, and apply a less strict set of clauses (e.g., we relax the pseudoboolean inequality). The solvers I've seen that implement incremental solver haven't (at least, by my reading) supported the kind of state-saving or rollback capability needed to handle this efficiently—and Picosat certainly doesn't—so we've simply had to feed a sequence of entirely independent SAT problems to the solver.

@msoos in https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415503020
> Ahhh... let me address all of your comments here, one by one. First, about rollback:
>
> > Thee solvers I've seen that implement incremental solver haven't (at least, by my reading) supported the kind of state-saving or rollback capability needed to handle this efficiently
> 
> So... there is rollback ;) Even in picoSAT, though probably I shouldn't tell you that :D The trick is the following. You have the core clauses:
> ```
> a V b V c
> -a V b
> ```
> Now you want to add new clauses that you may want to remove:
> ```
> f V g
> -f V e
> ```
> You have to do this:
> ```
> x V f V g
> x V -f V e
> ```
> and then you call the solver with:
> ```
> solve_with_assumptions(x = false)
> ```
> This will effectively force the original clauses to be used (since X is false, so the solver sees only the literals of the clauses you wanted to add). To remove the clauses, you simply add to the solver:
> ```
> add_clause(x = true)
> ```
> It's kinda tricky but cool. Where CMS excels is that it will do the *right thing* when it comes to assumed clauses and variables. Picosat would too from a correctness perspective, but CMS is a lot more scalable.
> 
> The gist of it is that you introduce a dummy variable, here called "x" and then you add this as a literal to every clause you may need to remove later. Then, you call `solve()` with the assumed literal `x=false`. What you have to take care of is not to introduce hundreds of these dummy variables. So if you have groups of clauses that you need to remove in groups, then each group of clauses should use the same dummy variable. This way, you can unroll, and you don't even need to unroll in a historical order. X could be for group 1, Y for group 2 and Z for group 3. Then you can solve(x=false, y=true, z=false) hence turning these groups of clauses on and off as you like :) If you add `x=true` then of course you delete that group of clauses for good. It's best to do that if you know you won't need them any longer (the solver will free the memory and will solve faster).
> 
> The above is all about (2), i.e. how you represent the problem. It seems we can potentially make a big difference here, adding a lot less clauses, potentially removing a lot of the problems related to the setup phase. Also note that if you do it this way, the solver *will keep memory of the work it has already done*. That can lead to an important performance impact as well. Could you take a shot at doing the above?

@msoos in https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415505531
> > I already saw that CMS has CMSat::Solver::save_state/CMSat::Solver::load_state which allows you to save/load some state information to/from a file
> 
> That is correct. It can actually do that, and it's a well-tested system, too. However, I would like to approach it from the perspective of the clause group switch on/off using dummy variables as per above. It would make your system work with any SAT solver, and it's a tried and tested method. The load/save is pretty cool and I could expose it to the outside, but it may have some restrictions -- I need to think through what exactly.
