issue_title: Conda is pulling the wrong `vc` when a package is unavailable in a preferred channel
issue_body:
@msarahan maybe you can help us here. As you can see from this log:

https://ci.appveyor.com/project/astrofrog/debug-conda-forge-appveyor/build/1.0.17#L82

even though that project is Python 2.7 and should be using `vc 9` conda is pulling a `qt` compiled with `vc 10`. Note that the project has `conda-forge` and `defaults` channels in that order and `conda-forge` does not have a `qt vc 9` version for that Arch. So conda should be pulling a `qt vc 9` from `defaults` instead.

If the channel order is resversed the right stack is downloaded.

IMO channel preference should not swicht `vc`s. I've seen a smiliar behavior when channel preference broke a package pinning in order to satisfy the channel order. Maybe `pinning`, like the `vc`s there, should preceed the channel order in the resolution in order to ensure a stable mix of packages.

cc @astrofrog

issue_title: conda build can't seem to download CRAN packages despite proxy settings
issue_body:
_From @mcg1969 on August 30, 2017 14:5_

Submitted on behalf of another user, but note that I have reproduced this myself on their system.

We're running conda-build 2.1.15 with requests 2.12.4 here. Steps to reproduce:
```
conda skeleton cran ldavis
conda build r-ldavis
```
During the build process, the CRAN package cannot be downloaded:
```
Source cache directory is: /opt/wakari/anaconda/conda-bld/src_cache
Downloading source to cache: LDAvis_0.3.2.tar.gz
Downloading https://cran.r-project.org/src/contrib/LDAvis_0.3.2.tar.gz
Error: HTTP None None for url <https://cran.r-project.org/src/contrib/LDAvis_0.3.2.tar.gz>
Elapsed: None

An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
HTTP errors are often intermittent, and a simple retry will get you on your way.
Downloading https://cran.r-project.org/src/contrib/Archive/LDAvis/LDAvis_0.3.2.tar.gz
Error: HTTP None None for url <https://cran.r-project.org/src/contrib/Archive/LDAvis/LDAvis_0.3.2.tar.gz>
Elapsed: None

An HTTP error occurred when trying to retrieve this URL.
HTTP errors are often intermittent, and a simple retry will get you on your way.
Could not download LDAvis_0.3.2.tar.gz
```
Using `wget` to download the package from the same URL works just fine, giving evidence that the proxy settings are correct.

The user reports that it worked before. He was running 2.1.7 at the time, but upgrading to 2.1.15 did not help. It's not clear then what could have changed from when it worked for him and when it did not, so it's possible the issue is environment-specific. Nevertheless, if wget is successfully able to navigate the proxy, it stands to reason that conda-build should be able to as well.

conda update --prefix /tmp/myconda --file compiled.txt

conda create --prefix /tmp/myconda --file compiled.txt

issue_title: TransportError documentation not consistent with code for ConnectionError
issue_body:
The documention for TransportError says:
```
    @property
    def info(self):
        """ Dict of returned error info from ES, where available. """
        return self.args[2]
```

But ConnectionError, that is a subclass of TransportError:
```
   def __str__(self):
        return 'ConnectionError(%s) caused by: %s(%s)' % (
            self.error, self.info.__class__.__name__, self.info)
```

`self.info.__class__.__name__` is not very useful with a dict.

issue_title: Statement completion for values is inconsistent, slow, and unpredictable
issue_body:
Statement completion of parameter values can be helpful when it works, but it is really slow and works for some parameter values and not others, making it impossible to predict when to use it and when not to.  As a result, users end up waiting several seconds to get parameter values that may never be shown.

If we're going to do this, we need to make sure the behavior is intuitable and consistent.

issue_title: Deployment template launch fail missing error code or reason
issue_body:
We found that when deployment template launch failed, the exception doesn't provide error code or reason.

Here is snippet of code:

        try:
            result = resource_client.deployments.create_or_update(
                    resource_group,
                    deployment_name,
                    properties=DeploymentProperties(
                        mode=DeploymentMode.incremental,
                        template=template_json,
                        parameters=param_json,
                    )
                ).result()

        except Exception as e:
            reason = str(e)
            self.logger.error(reason)
            raise ConnectToLegacyVNetErr(reason)

So the exception we captured was “Long running operation failed with status 'Failed'”. This doesn’t tell us exactly why the template deployment failed. Is there way to capture the reason of deployment failure?

Thanks.
Li

issue_body:
### Description:
I would like to use MD5 validation to ensure my data survives transport when I upload blobs to Azure storage. Per the documentation, I supply a content-MD5 header along with my upload, so that the service validates the incoming file against this header.

This works great for small files. **But once I try to upload files that require chunked uploads -- e.g. I'm currently trying to upload a 1GB file -- the validation is always unsuccessful.** I'm using `BlobService.put_block_blob_from_path(...)` for the upload.

For reference, here is how I calculate the MD5 locally and pass it along with my upload.

```
# md5 generation for validating uploads
import hashlib
def md5_for_file(f, block_size=2**20):
    md5 = hashlib.md5()
    while True:
        data = f.read(block_size)
        if not data:
            break
        md5.update(data)
    return md5.digest().encode('base64')[:-1] 
    
# do the uploads, with file path p
for p in paths:
    blob_name = path_leaf(p)
    original_md5 = md5_for_file(open(p, 'rb'))
    blob_service.put_block_blob_from_path(storage_container_name, blob_name, p, content_md5=original_md5) # specify md5 to do an md5 check automatically
```

I get a WindowsAzureError when I run the above, due to the MD5 not validating properly:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "azureUpload.py", line 44, in <module>
    blob_service.put_block_blob_from_path(storage_container_name, blob_name, p, content_md5=original_md5) # specify md5 to do an md5 check automatically
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/blobservice.py", line 764, in put_block_blob_from_path
    progress_callback)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/blobservice.py", line 861, in put_block_blob_from_file
    x_ms_lease_id)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/blobservice.py", line 696, in put_blob
    self._perform_request(request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/storageclient.py", line 150, in _perform_request
    _storage_error_handler(ex)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/storage/__init__.py", line 889, in _storage_error_handler
    return _general_error_handler(http_error)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/azure/__init__.py", line 931, in _general_error_handler
    http_error.respbody.decode('utf-8'))
azure.WindowsAzureError
```

# The issue

My local MD5 computation isn't the issue -- I've checked it against Azure with small uploads, and it produces the same result as md5sum for my 1GB file.

**How should big files be uploaded with MD5 validation?** It seems that the API should be changed so that MD5 is computed for each chunk.

# How to reproduce

Create a >64MB file using `dd if=/dev/urandom of=65M.random bs=1M count=65`, then upload it with the code above. You get the same traceback as I did.

But if you chop 20MB off that using `dd if=/dev/urandom of=45M.random bs=1M count=45`, we won't trigger chunking, and the upload succeeds, along with MD5 validation.

If you remove MD5 validation (don't specify `content_md5` parameter), both uploads succeed.

Thus, the issue lies with MD5 validation with files that trigger chunking.

##### Original Issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/264
### Comments (2):
#### @huguesv commented on 2015-01-05 15:57:16:
> Thanks for letting us know about this issue.

The documentation for Put Block List REST API says:
"Note that this header is associated with the request content, and not with the content of the blob itself."

So passing in the MD5 of the whole blob to Put Block List is definitely the wrong thing to do.

We should calculate the MD5 for each chunk and pass it to Put Block (Put Blob in the case of no chunking), if the user wants validation.  The content\_md5 parameter should be changed to a Boolean, matching the behavior of the useTransactionalMD5 parameter used in Node.js SDK:
https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-node/blob/master/lib/services/blob/blobservice.js

All Page Blob and Block Blob APIs should be reviewed for this, both upload and download.


#### @maximz commented on 2015-01-06 18:44:13:
> @huguesv Thanks for the update. Let me know if there's any further way I can help. (For now, I've just disabled MD5 verification in my code)

issue_title: Private key recognition hook is not accurate
issue_body:
Hi Team! 

I just discovered your work, and it is fantastic. Many thanks for putting this together, it is very useful.

I have a use case where code is extracting private keys, and contains lines such as

```
String keyContents = base64Content.VersionData.tostring();
keyContents = keyContents.replace('-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----', '');
keyContents = keyContents.replace('-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----', '');
keyContents = keyContents.replace('\n', '');
```
This is caught by the private key discoverer and it makes the tests fail. 

Technically, private keys or certs would have a pattern that is 
(-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----)( base64 stuff)(-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----)

The current code seems to just check if the words "BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY" appears in the code, which is slightly weak. 

As a result, I am using a combination of pre-commit and [Talisman](https://github.com/thoughtworks/talisman) but I would love to have a single code base for everything. 

Best,
Sam

## Long story short

Trying to use aiohttp-2.2.3 on a host with only Python 3.5 fails because the wheel includes a C extension linked against Python 3.4 libraries. This is the case for all aiohttp-2.x.x-cp35-cp35m-manylinux1_x86_64 wheels at pypi.

## Expected behaviour

C extensions should only be linked against the version of Python the wheel is built against.

## Actual behaviour

The following wheels include aiohttp/_http_parser.cpython-34m.so which is linked to libpython3.4m.so.1.0:
- [2.2.0](https://pypi.python.org/packages/2b/b8/f8331846fc582bb8c24c0425cfa32bf5d00446be080316cf2a7296fbd6e0/aiohttp-2.2.0-cp35-cp35m-manylinux1_x86_64.whl#md5=c1b4d78f0874fc4a168e8ce8a2708121)

issue_title: response.text() crash if body contain non UTF-8 symbol
issue_body:
## Long story short
Unfortunately the world is not perfect. 
Server in body UTF-8 can return non UTF-8 character.

Content-Type:      text/html; charset=utf-8

<a href="/те�нополь">

Error: utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0x80 in position 19404: invalid start byte

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->

## Expected behaviour

<!-- What is the behaviour you expect? -->

## Actual behaviour

<!-- What's actually happening? -->

## Steps to reproduce

<!-- Please describe steps to reproduce the issue.
     If you have a script that does that please include it here within
     markdown code markup -->

## Your environment

<!-- Describe the environment you have that lead to your issue.
     This includes project version, OS, proxy server and other bits that
     are related to your case. -->

## Long story short

I'm trying to make my test to run on the same port instead of a random port. The reason is that I'm making integration tests and I need it to receive calls from another service that I can't really configure the port easily.

My idea is that I need to make a class FixedPortTestServer that will inherit from TestServer and override the `start_server` method to force a specific port.

Then I need to pass this instance to the TestClient initialization. The AioHTTPTestCase use the result of `get_application` and give it to TestClient init parameters. This makes me think that I can make `get_application` return the FixedPortTestServer instance instead of the application instance:

```python
    async def get_application(self):
        return FixedPortTestServer(my_app)
```

Which is wrong... Now I have a properly initialized TestClient using my TestServer but `self.app` in the AioHTTPTestCase give me the TestServer instead of my application which doesn't make any sense.

## Expected behaviour

I expect to get the application from `self.app` within a test method of AioHTTPTestCase and I want to be able to override the TestServer instance with whatever BaseTestServer instance I want without needing to override a private method.

## Actual behaviour

Currently, my only way to override the TestServer instance in the initialization of the TestClient is to override the private method `_get_client` in the AioHTTPTestCase.

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import asyncio
import socket
from aiohttp.helpers import sentinel
from aiohttp.test_utils import AioHTTPTestCase, TestServer, unittest_run_loop
from yarl import URL

from mymodule import myapp


class FixedPortTestServer(TestServer):
    @asyncio.coroutine
    def start_server(self, loop=None, **kwargs):
        if self.server:
            return
        self._loop = loop
        self._socket = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        self._socket.bind((self.host, 8080)) # force port
        self.port = self._socket.getsockname()[1]
        self._ssl = kwargs.pop('ssl', None)
        if self.scheme is sentinel:
            if self._ssl:
                scheme = 'https'
            else:
                scheme = 'http'
            self.scheme = 'http'
        self._root = URL('{}://{}:{}'.format(self.scheme,
                                             self.host,
                                             self.port))

        handler = yield from self._make_factory(**kwargs)
        self.server = yield from self._loop.create_server(
            handler, ssl=self._ssl, sock=self._socket)


class MyTestCase(AioHTTPTestCase):
    async def get_application(self):
        return FixedPortTestServer(myapp)

    @unittest_run_loop
    async def test_initialization(self):
        self.assertIs(self.app, myapp)
```

## Your environment

Python 3.6

issue_title: Option to include all directories when downloading specific files
issue_body:
Feature Request:
> When I download specified files, it creates a main directory containing the project (with the project name) and then just the necessary subdirectories to fill in the requested file.  Would it be possible, or is there already a way, in which I could request specific files, but it would create the entire subdirectory tree of the project, just creating empty folders when none of the files from that folder are being selected for download?

There is currently no mechanism to download all directories when using the download command with the `--include <path>` argument.

If we were to add an option to include all directories(folders) we would need to add the check to PathFilteredProject:
https://github.com/Duke-GCB/DukeDSClient/blob/f4c192dfd6657a8d6f85ccb3050d4c9c936bfa25/ddsc/core/pathfilter.py#L161-L167

Need to figure out if this flag should/could have any effect when used with the exclude option.

A user requested to see what would be uploaded before running the upload process.
We could add a --dryrun flag:

```
ddsclient upload -p MouseProject results/ --dryrun
```

This would print out what files will be uploaded.
We have a summary that already prints for regular upload.

Here's the actual loss, `bk == autograd.numpy` here, `data` and `theta` are `namedtuple` (the packing-unpacking part works just fine with `autograd` here, tested with other losses):

    def predict(self, theta, data):
        const = assert_arr_shape({
            data.X.shape: ('batch_size', self.n_steps, self.n_input_dim),
            data.y.shape: ('batch_size', self.n_hidden_dim)
        })
        h_prev = bk.repeat(theta.h_0[:, bk.newaxis], const.batch_size, axis=1).T
        activation = lambda x: bk.maximum(0, 1-x)
        to_stack = []
        for i in range(self.n_steps):
            input2hidden = bk.dot(data.X[:, i, :], theta.W_input_to_hidden)
            hidden2hidden = bk.dot(h_prev, theta.W_hidden_to_hidden)
            before_activation = (input2hidden + hidden2hidden)
            h_prev = activation(before_activation)
            to_stack.append(h_prev)
        output = bk.stack(to_stack, axis=1)
        assert_arr_shape({output.shape: (None, self.n_steps, self.n_hidden_dim)})
        
        return output

    def loss(self, theta, data):
        pred = self.predict(theta, data)
        last_step_pred = pred[:, -1, :]
        loss = bk.sum(bk.abs(last_step_pred - data.y))
        return loss

The forward pass (`loss(x)`, `x`-ravel'ed everything) executes just fine, but when I try computing the gradient (`autograd.grad(loss)(x)`), this exception raises.

It does indeed tries to apply `abs()` on the np.ndarray that has `FloatNode` objects instead of actual floats in it.

Thank you!

Ben

issue_title: Support for multiple addresses within the blockstack wallet
blockstackd: move subdomain DB to blockstackd
issue_body:
Right now, `blockstack_client` lazily builds up the `subdomains.db` file when it processes a subdomain query.  Instead, `blockstackd` should eagerly build up this database as it receives zone files.

In particular, we should treat subdomains like normal names whenever possible.

The URL schema doesn't allow `@` to be in paths, whilst it's [allowed according to spec](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986#section-3.3).

cc @kantai

KMIP protocol supports batching operations, for example to execute the sequence locate/check/get in a single request. Is request batching supported by PyKMIP? I did not find any mention of this either way in the documentation. I see in KMIPProxy there is some support for batching but it is not clear if it works. For instance `create_key_pair` has a boolean batch parameter, which if true appends the batch item to `self.batch_items` instead of sending it immediately. But I can't find any way of causing the client to send these items in a batch.

It seems like (if batching were supported) KMIPProxy would have batch params on all of its functions, and each would do something like this:

```
batch_item = self._build_xxx_batch_item(...)
self.batch_items.append(batch_item)
if batch == True:
   return
request = self._build_request_message(credential, self.batch_items)
self.batch_items = []
response = self._send_and_receive_message(request) 
```

So that if you called several functions with batch=True they would be stored and then when the final command with batch=False is issued all of the previously batched commands are also sent for execution.

Is this totally off base? Maybe batching is supported and I am just misunderstanding something about how it works in PyKMIP? Any hints most appreciated.

issue_title: Add descriptions of tools to documentation
issue_body:
As an example:

A narrative description of the method with versions (and cites, which I haven't included here):
FastQC v0.11.5 was used to obtain quality metrics on each FASTQ input file. Reads were aligned by STAR v 020201 using indices generated from the human reference genome GRCh38 and the human gene models Gencode 23. The alignments were piped to Samblaster 0.1.22 for duplicate marking and samtools v0.1.19 for sorting. The tools geneBody_coverage.py and read_distribution.py from RSeQC v2.7.10 were run on all bams. 

Reference data sources:
GRCh38 was downloaded from ftp://ftp.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genomes/archive/old_genbank/Eukaryotes/vertebrates_mammals/Homo_sapiens/GRCh38/seqs_for_alignment_pipelines/GCA_000001405.15_GRCh38_no_alt_analysis_set.fna.gz on July 22, 2016
Gencode 23 was downloaded from  ftp://ftp.sanger.ac.uk/pub/gencode/Gencode_human/release_23/gencode.v23.annotation.gtf.gz July 22, 2016

Command details:
FastQC is run with the --no-groups argument.
Star indices were generated with the following command.
STAR  --runThreadN 8 --runMode genomeGenerate --genomeDir GCA_000001405.15_GRCh38_no_alt_AND_gencodev23 --genomeFastaFiles $fa --sjdbGTFfile $gtf
Star alignments were generated and duplicates were marked with the following command. 
STAR --readFilesCommand gunzip -c --genomeLoad LoadAndKeep --outSAMunmapped Within KeepPairs --readFilesIn ${e1Files} ${e2Files}  --genomeDir $refDir --runThreadN $threads --outStd SAM --outFileNamePrefix ${sample} | samblaster  | samtools view -Sb - > ${outDir}${sample}.md.bam

Warning when try to create DalivikVMFormat object, file attached.
issue_body:
I did came across a some apk files with which androguard is throwing warnings, but I am not sure whether it is because of androguard or because of  corrupt or invalid files. Please check the attached file.
[7f1b7ae2859bad220941924e2adea612.zip](https://github.com/androguard/androguard/files/1296322/7f1b7ae2859bad220941924e2adea612.zip)

To produce the error, remove the zip extension(just changed it for uploading):
`apk = APK("7f1b7ae2859bad220941924e2adea612")`

`Dvmformat = DalvikVMFormat(apk.get_dex())`

The message is:
`WARNING: unknown string item @ 0x2546(56)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/androguard-3.1.0-py2.7.egg/androguard/core/bytecodes/dvm.py", line 7285, in get_string
    return self.__strings_off[off].get()
KeyError: 9542
`

The workflow has a little bit changed, we should document those changes!

When you are working with an APK, you should not use DalvikVMFormat directly as it does not contain all the information. You should always use Analysis object, which contains all VMs and can produce nicer outputs. 
For example, if you need all classes do not use:
```
#d = DalvikVMFormat(...)
d.get_classes()
```
but:
```
#dx = Analysis(...)
#dx.add(DalvikVMFormat(...))
dx.get_classes()
```

DalvikVMFormat should be the basis for Analysis - which contains the raw data. Of course, the pure DalvikVMFormat objects might be of interest as well, but you can still get them and use them.

Cannot instantiate a QApplication.

```python
import sys
from PyQt5 import QtWidgets

app = QtWidgets.QApplication(sys.argv)
```

Results in:

```bat
This application failed to start because it could not find or load the Qt platfo
rm plugin "windows".

Available platform plugins are: minimal (from C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\PyQt
5\plugins\platforms), offscreen (from C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\PyQt5\plugin
s\platforms), windows (from C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\PyQt5\plugins\platform
s).

Reinstalling the application may fix this problem.
```

Having encountered the problem in the past, I could solve it by appending the plugins by hand like this:

```python
import os
import sys

import PyQt5

dirname = os.path.dirname(PyQt5.__file__)
plugin_path = os.path.join(dirname, 'plugins', 'platforms')
os.environ['QT_QPA_PLATFORM_PLUGIN_PATH'] = plugin_path

from PyQt5 import QtWidgets
app = QtWidgets.QApplication(sys.argv)
```

However this produces the same error. The odd thing is that it says it can't load the plugin, and then lists the plugin as one of the available ones. Maybe a permission issue? Yet permissions on the files are unlocked and even replacing it with the source original from the Qt installation yields the same results. Removing the plugin from the directory correctly removes it from plugins found.

### depends.exe

Running depends on qwindows.dll yields a few interesting results.

![depends](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2152766/4316602/e6c69332-3f08-11e4-9ab5-9f4c38e611cb.PNG)

However each of these are available directly within the PyQt5 Python package directory.

### It works

If keeping the original installation of Qt on the PATH, PyQt5 seems to work. From a terminal:

```python
# Keeping C:\Qt\Qt5.3.0\5.3\msvc2013_64\bin on the PATH

import os
import sys

from PyQt5 import QtWidgets
app = QtWidgets.QApplication(sys.argv)
button = QtWidgets.QPushButton("Hello World")
button.show()
```

So there must be something within this directory which is requited by PyQt5. But copying the entire contents into the PyQt5 Python library directory yields no better results.

# Issue Title

Add `Microsoft.SqlServer.Replication` (Microsoft.SqlServer.Rmo.dll)

# General

We host SQL server in an enterprise environment where database servers are taking offline randomly. Currently we have a master DB server which is replicated to a number of slave servers. If the master DB server goes down, then we manually promote one of the slaves to master, along with updating the configuration of applications that depend on them.

Given the pain in this and the added downtime, we're looking to build a tool that does this automatically, and it seems as though the .NET Framework library `Microsoft.SqlServer.Replication` should help.

However we're moving everything to .Net Core, so using this library is a bit of a step backwards. Is adding this library to .Net Core on the roadmap?

Note: I see that there is https://www.nuget.org/packages/Unofficial.Microsoft.SQLServer.RMO, but as it's not open source, has minimal usage, and hasn't been updated for a year, I'm unwilling to use it.

See:

- https://github.com/dotnet/core/issues/711#issuecomment-312688873
- https://github.com/dotnet/core/issues/711#issuecomment-312708156

/cc @rrelyea

issue_title: Bind event on injected object (PropertyChanged is always null)
issue_body:
In an .Net Core WebAPI, I am injecting an custom `IWritableOptions `object (derived from `IOptionsSnapshot`) through the constructor of a service that I configured via the `ConfigureServices `method. This object has an even receiver that fires every time a property is changed on that object. Inside my service, I subscribe to the event of the object passed through the constructor. 

Then, I have a controller that also gets an `IWritableOptions `object passed through the constructor via DI. I use this controller to change the value of the property for which I configured an event receiver. 

So far so good. When I use the controller to change the values of my object, however, the event isn't triggered. I suspect that the runtime is instantiating multiple instances of my `IWritableOptions ` class and that the event isn't triggered because of that.

Can anybody clarify if this is possible?

Links:

Repository that demonstrates what I am trying to do: [Link](https://github.com/LeonidasFettikus/EventDI)
Implementation of my service class: [Link](https://www.stevejgordon.co.uk/asp-net-core-2-ihostedservice)
Implementation of my `IWritableOptions` class: [Link](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40970944/how-to-update-values-into-appsetting-json)

In the repository, the relevant code is in ClientService.cs. `PropertyChanged `is never triggered here.

issue_title: Missing creation of StringComparer for other languages
issue_body:
I'm trying to retarget a portable C# library for .Net core 1.0.

I used to do something like this to get a string comparer for a different language, once I obtained a CultureInfo object for the language (via a delegate set by the caller):
IComparer<T> comparer = (IComparer<T>)StringComparer.Create(cultureInfo, caseInsensitive);

.Net Core currently seems to only support it for the current culture via properties like StringComparer.CurrentCulture.

Is there a way to get a string comparer for a given language or culture?

This is important for an app that uses multiple languages simultaneously, such as an app for language-learning.

On arch linux. Tried text mode, manual/standalone authenticators, they all hang during authentication after it's shown that an IP queried the token:

```
How would you like to authenticate with the Let's Encrypt CA?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: Manual Authenticator (manual)
2: Standalone Authenticator (standalone)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate number [1-2] then [enter] (press 'c' to cancel): 2
Please enter in your domain name(s) (comma and/or space separated)  (Enter 'c'
to cancel):leclan.ch www.leclan.ch
66.133.109.36 - - [26/Oct/2015 21:44:21] "GET /.well-known/acme-challenge/... HTTP/1.1" 200 -
```1

Right now the `--manual` plugin requires user interaction, but there are some use cases where it needn't. For example, when using `--manual` with Google App Engine, one can [automate that entire process](https://brianmhunt.github.io/articles/let-s-encrypt-acme-on-appengine/), but it's quite convoluted. The process could be vastly simplified if, instead of using manual input, the `--manual` plugin took a shell command, that I would imagine could work as follows: 1. User calls `letsencrypt --manual --shell-command "./upload-script --server=example.com" -d example.com -d www.example.com` 2. Let's Encrypt calls `upload-script`, and pipes the challenges to the standard input, something like: 
 example.com challenge-filename-1 challenge-1 
 www.example.com challenge-filename-2 challenge-2
It would be trivial to generalize this with another argument like `--shell-pipe-format` with options such as `csv`, `tsv`, `json`, etc. 3. Once the shell command completes Let's Encrypt runs the challenge. --- ### Example In the case of Google App Engine, a simplistic `upload-script` might look like (and my bash is a little rusty, but I hope it conveys the point): ```bash # Write the challenge files to a place where AppEngine will read/publish them CHALLENGE_PATH = "app/acme/challenges" while read line do CHALLENGE=$line[2] CHALLENGE_FILE="$CHALLENGE_PATH$line[1]" echo CHALLENGE > CHALLENGE_FILE while done < "${1:-/dev/stdin//:/ }" # Publish to AppEngine appcfg.py update app ``` Once the script completes (and presuming that `acme/challenges` publishes to `./well-known/...`), Let's Encrypt should validate successfully against the App Engine. ---- I find this nicely abstracts the validation process. Compare this to [the fragile monstrosities I have created to accomplish the same](https://gist.github.com/brianmhunt/7c647dce3e43d886f1d2#file-regen-py) that carefully read and interpret the output of Let's Encrypt. (In retrospect my time would have been better applied writing a patch for or alternative to `--manual` – sorry I missed that opportunity; I hope to return to it as time permits, but I wanted to take the time share this in case someone else might be able to pick it up)
There are still a number of references to https://letsencrypt.readthedocs.org in our code. These should be cleaned up to point to the new doc location.

issue_title: Errors on subsequent runs if /etc/letsencrypt or /var/lib/letsencrypt are created with a conservative umask
issue_body:
I ran into a problem with `sudo venv/bin/letsencrypt --authenticator apache auth` or similar after the first time I run it (from the letsencrypt.log file):
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "venv/bin/letsencrypt", line 9, in <module>
    load_entry_point('letsencrypt==0.1', 'console_scripts', 'letsencrypt')()
  File "/home/aetherkn/src/letsencrypt/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/cli.py", line 736, in main
    directory, constants.CONFIG_DIRS_MODE, os.geteuid())
  File "/home/aetherkn/src/letsencrypt/venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/letsencrypt/le_util.py", line 94, in make_or_verify_dir
    "%s exists, this client can't access it" % directory)
Error: /etc/letsencrypt exists, this client can't access it
```
This surprised me because it successfully created both /etc/letsencrypt and /var/lib/letsencrypt after the first run. However, upon investigating, I noticed that the default file mask for creating the directories by `make_or_verify_dir()`  was 0755:

```
def make_or_verify_dir(directory, mode=0o755, uid=0):
  ...
```
And the directories were being created with 0700:
```
$ ls -ld /etc/letsencrypt /var/lib/letsencrypt
drwx------ 5 root root 4096 Sep  9 22:03 /etc/letsencrypt
drwx------ 2 root root 4096 Sep  9 21:58 /var/lib/letsencrypt
```
This caused `check_permissions()` to return false because the permissions were not exactly 0755. In my standard bash configuration I use a umask of 0077 in order to default to denying group and other users form having any permissions on newly created files, and sudo inherits my umask. However, `make_or_verify_dir()` and `check_permissions()` do not take this into account when checking to ensure that the main directories are properly configured.

I would suggest modifying the permissions check to ensure that other users cannot modify the directories, instead of checking for an exact file mode number.

issue_title: Exceptions don't have enough information for users
issue_body:
As a Developer _I want_ to have `code`, `message`, `description` whenever a Watson service raises an exception _so that_ I can write code that reacts different depending on the exception.

# Context
It seems like the Python exceptions this SDK is throwing are not very useful for developers:
> **Bruce Adams:**
> I do not have a strong opinion about exception classes. I do find it irritating that `WatsonException` takes a structured error response from as service call and flattens it into a string as the exception args. When I want to make decisions based on the error code (my code needs to react differently to 429 compared to a 500), I have to pick the code out of the string.


More info:
 * https://stackoverflow.com/questions/256222/which-exception-should-i-raise-on-bad-illegal-argument-combinations-in-python
 * https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=o9pEzgHorH0
## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Switch to use `ValueError` when there is a missing or illegal argument
- [ ] Parse the error parameters and make them available to users (`code`, `description`)

issue_title: Only single path added for Flask API MethodViews
issue_body:
The [Flask documentation](http://flask.pocoo.org/docs/0.12/views/#method-views-for-apis) gives a use case - for RESTful APIs - where a single method view can be added under multiple URL rules. The following code snippet is provided as an example.

```python
class UserAPI(MethodView):

    def get(self, user_id):
        if user_id is None:
            # return a list of users
            pass
        else:
            # expose a single user
            pass

    def post(self):
        # create a new user
        pass

    def delete(self, user_id):
        # delete a single user
        pass

    def put(self, user_id):
        # update a single user
        pass

user_view = UserAPI.as_view('user_api')
app.add_url_rule('/users/', defaults={'user_id': None},
                 view_func=user_view, methods=['GET',])
app.add_url_rule('/users/', view_func=user_view, methods=['POST',])
app.add_url_rule('/users/<int:user_id>', view_func=user_view,
                 methods=['GET', 'PUT', 'DELETE'])
```

If we define the following apispec and add the above view function like so

```python
spec = APISpec(
    title='Swagger Petstore',
    version='1.0.0',
    plugins=[
        'apispec.ext.flask',
        'apispec.ext.marshmallow',
    ],
)

with app.test_request_context():
    spec.add_path(view=user_view)
```

only one of the above paths would be included in the generated apispec.

Looking at the `apispec.ext.flask` module, it seems that this behavior is a result of line 92 in the `_rule_for_view` function.

```python
    # WARNING: Assume 1 rule per view function for now
    rule = current_app.url_map._rules_by_endpoint[endpoint][0]
```

Given the comment, it seems like this behavior is expected. Is there any interest in/intent to modify this to enable all of the paths to be added to the apispec in the above situation?

In my project I was making use of the Meta field type to include resource object level meta information about the object. This was especially important when the primary data was a list of resource objects.

```"python"
{'data': [{'attributes': {'name': 'Impossible Stand'},
          'id': '21',

issue_body:
In #475 @thunderfish24 asked

> Is the statement about ModelChain here still valid: "CEC module specifications and the single diode model are not yet supported."?

The statement is not valid and should be fixed. Thanks @thunderfish24 for pointing this out.

Problem

We use Swagger for API documentation. https://github.com/marcgibbons/django-rest-swagger. There are two problems when trying to use this package in conjunction with Swagger. 1) Since the package enforces the media-type 'application/vnd.api+json', it will cause Swagger to throw an error. `{"errors":{"detail":"Could not satisfy the request Accept header."}}` I've worked around this in the past using custom content negotiation. ![screen shot 2015-08-10 at 4 08 12 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9755598/9182845/bd063bb6-3f79-11e5-9d03-0255dc53fa3a.png) 2) Provided the first issue is fixed, Swagger hangs with a fetching resource list message. ![screen shot 2015-08-10 at 4 12 21 pm](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/9755598/9182945/56c1b2a8-3f7a-11e5-913b-c743b6940ea9.png)

Potential Solutions

I haven't dug into this much, but one thing I've noticed is that this package is looking for `views` to have an action. I assume this means even SwaggerViews need an action. If I add an action to the SwaggerView, it still hangs.

issue_title: Run IDs generated incorrectly when SQLObserver is the first or only observer.
issue_body:
In the SQL Observer, the run IDs are stored as strings, not as integers.  When the SQL Observer is asked to generate a new ID, it sorts the IDs in the run table, takes the last one, and then returns that value +1.

If it starts at 0, this causes it to generate a non-unique ID on the 12th run.  The problem is that, because the column is text, not numeric, it sorts the IDs lexicographically, not numeritcally.  The values 0..10 sorted lexicographically are:

0
1
10
2
...
9

Thus, on the 12th run, the SQL Observer generates the ID, "10" a second time, which is a non-unique ID, causing the run to fail.

issue_title: Any way to specify _id to change the file path used by FileStorage Observer?
issue_body:
When I use FileStorage Observer, I notice that every time I run my program, it saves information in a new folder named by incremental numbers, for example, 
**/ExpName/1/run.json**
**/ExpName/2/run.json**
**/ExpName/3/run.json**
But I hope I can specify the folder name by myself. 
I have thoroughly read the source codes but can't find any way that I can specify it. 
I think the folder name is determined by `run._id`. But even I change `run._id` during program running, it doesn't affect the folder path. 
How should I do to change the folder name?

Notebooks have quickly become and excellent framework for interactive experimentation. It would be great to have support in `sacred` for running experiments in notebook cells. At the moment the experiment object can not even be created because the notebook code cell does not have a filename, which `sacred` depends on.

Senza's TaupageAutoScalingGroup should validate application_id and application_version as we do in Kio (https://github.com/zalando-stups/kio/blob/master/resources/api/kio-api.yaml#L482).

* application_id must match pattern: "^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*[a-z0-9]$"
* application_version must match pattern: "^[A-Za-z0-9](?:[A-Za-z0-9._-]*[A-Za-z0-9])?$"

Background: sometimes users accidentally put the full Docker image (pierone.example.org/../..:123) into application_version

issue_title: Senza scale should ask when scaling multiple versions
issue_body:
Yesterday, I was scaling a stack with two versions and executed:
```
$ senza scale STACKNAME 0
```
this scaled **BOTH** versions of the stack to 0 instances.

**My expectation** was that `scale` would behave the same as `delete` in that it asks for confirmation when scaling a stack with multiple versions present.

To prevent accidently deletion of a stack, it would be safer to promt for a confirmation.
For scripted usage, a --force option to skip the confirmation would be helpful.

issue_title: Z3_goal API breaks invariant I expected to hold
issue_body:
This is a minor issue.

I was playing with Z3's goal API over the weekend and I noticed that an invariant I expected to hold wasn't.

I expected that when I add a formula to a goal (via `Z3_goal_assert()`) the number of formulas in the goal (as reported by `Z3_goal_size()`) to increment by one.

This does not appear to be the case. It seems that the goal eagerly modifies inserted formulas before any tactic is applied to it.

This is what I've observed so far.

* If the inserted formula is a logical `and`  it gets split into separate formula (i.e. the goal size is incremented by `n` for a `n-ary` logical `and`, instead of by 1).
* If `true` is inserted into the goal it is ignored (i.e. increment is 0).
* If `false` is inserted into the goal all existing formulas are removed from the goal and the goal will only contain `false`. Attempting to add more formulas to this goal from now on does nothing.

From the user's perspective I wasn't expecting this to happen. I was expecting Z3 to add the formulas I asked to be inserted into the goal verbatim. I was expecting these changes to be made once a tactic was applied to the goal, not before.

Either the behaviour of `Z3_goal`s needs to be change or it should be documented what `Z3_goal_assert()` will do. My guess is changing the documentation would be the better option.

issue_title: "Overflow encountered when expanding vector" during solving
issue_body:
I have several queries where Z3 aborts with `Overflow encountered when expanding vector`. In some cases this error comes after only a few seconds for not-too-big formulas. I am currently using Z3 4.5.0-124-gc1480b4, but I can reproduce this with the latest git revision.

So I am wondering whether
- this might be a bug that leads to unexpectedly high memory usage?
- something could be optimized to solve such formulas with less memory? The query actually does not seem to be that difficult: If I serialize the solver and give the resulting SMTLIB2 formula to Z3, it can be solved instantly.
- Z3 could be changed to allow the use of larger vectors? I have a 64bit system and would be fine with Z3 taking a few more gigabytes for solving the formula (I am currently using a memory limit of 15 GB, and Z3 just uses 4 and then gives up).

The queries where I see this problem all use linear arithmetic over Ints and UFs, some of them also use arrays and/or quantifiers. Interestingly, I have not encountered this problem for bitvector queries so far.
I cannot remember seeing this problem earlier, it looks like it is related to a change in Z3 during the last months?

Here is an [archive](http://students.fim.uni-passau.de/~wendler/z3-overflow-encountered.tar.bz2) with a replay log and the same query as serialized solver output for one of my problems. If you need more information, just tell me.

issue_title: propagate-bv-bounds tactic consuming large amounts of memory, possibly stuck in a loop
issue_body:
I'm using the `propagate-bv-bounds` tactic as a pre-processing step and I've encountered a benchmark that causes Z3 to consumes huge amounts (> 20GiB) of memory and CPU time when I try to apply this tactic.

The benchmark is [apply-intSqRoot.smt2.zip](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/1075780/apply-intSqRoot.smt2.zip). This is taken from the SMTLIB QF_BV benchmark suite.

The benchmark is `QF_BV/bmc-bv/intSqRoot.smt2` from the  SMTLIB QF_BV benchmark suite but I've replaced `(check-sat)` with `(apply (and-then simplify propagate-bv-bounds))`.

Is this expected behaviour?

issue_title: Using z3 java library in windows
issue_body:
I'm able to use z3 in OSX by import the jar file (com.microsoft.z3) into my eclipse java project and set its native library location to the folder that contains the libz3java.dylib file. However, problems pop up when I was trying to port my project to windows system. I added all the library files in the bin folder of z3's 4.5.0-x64-win release into the jar's native library location but still got the following error: 

**Exception in thread "main" java.lang.UnsatisfiedLinkError: ...\libz3java.dll: Can't find dependent libraries**

According to Dependency Walker, it seems that many system libraries (name starts with API-MS-WIN-CORE or EXT-MS-WIN-GDI) are missing.

I also tried adding the directory containing libz3.dll and libz3java.dll to my system's PATH instead of java.library.path as @wintersteiger suggested in issue #1064 but it didn't work.

I'm not sure what to do. Am I supposed to manually find all those missing DLL in my system and add them to the java.library.path? Thanks for your help in advance.

issue_title: Weird behaviour of "+" operation over BitVectors
issue_body:
Not sure if I hit a bug in Z3 or Z3Py (4.4.2) or I just miss something.
Here is a working example.
I coded Sudoku problem as an example.
How would I detect if all 9 numbers in row are unique?
Yes, there is "Distinct", but before I got to know about it, I made this equation:

`1<<cell1 | 1<<cell2 ... | 1<<cell9 == 0b1111111110 (binary)`

So if 9 input cells has 9 unique numbers, the result of this expression should be
0b1111111110 (all cells has numbers in rage of 1..9, hence the last zero bit).

So far so good, I've encoded this and it works very well: http://pastebin.com/arFLmbeb
Now what is weird is that if I replace OR operation with PLUS...

`1<<cell1 + 1<<cell2 ... + 1<<cell9 == 0b1111111110 (binary)`

... it doesn't works, although, mathematically, everything should work: http://pastebin.com/AtAwk3tx

Do I miss something? Should I use some specific tactic?

Thanks in advance!

The problem shown up on MacOSX Yosemite 10.10.4 where I'm getting a C error outside the Java VM, apparently some dynamic memory allocation problem, that crashes my whole application. The problem has been reported on stackoverflow here:

There is a Homebrew `z3` package, but it does not include the OCaml bindings. It would be useful for me if there was a Homebrew package called e.g. `z3-ocaml` with the OCaml bindings.

BTW: It seems that it would be easier to create such a package if it were possible to build the Z3 OCaml bindings without having to build Z3 itself. That is, it would be nice if there was a way to build the Z3 OCaml bindings against an installed Z3 DLL instead of against a just-built Z3 DLL. (Unlike the Debian package manager, Homebrew does not seem to support generating multiple binary packages from the same build.)

My formula is:

```
ForAll([x0, x1],
Implies(Or(x0 != 0, x1 != 0),
    And(P0*x0*x0 + P1*x0*x1 + P2*x0*x1 + P3*x1*x1 > 0,
        -2*P0*x0*x1 + P1*x0*x0 - P1*x0*x1 - P1*x1*x1 + P2*x0*x0 - P2*x0*x1 - P2*x1*x1 + 2*P3*x0*x1 - 2*P3*x1*x1 < 0)))
```


Below is a sample python script that asks Z3 to find some reals P0, .., P3 for which the formula is true.

A solution exists and is `(1.5, -0.5, -0.5, 1)`. With P0, .., P3 to be synthesized, Z3 believes it is `unsat`.  

When the tuple is `(1.5, -0.5, -0.5, 1)`, with `Q(., .)` or with `RealVal(.)`, the formula is satisfied.

Here's the output to the console:
```
P* variables unsat
hard coded float sat
[]
P* Q sat
[]
P* RealVal sat
[]
```

issue_title: [FTPFS] Consider restructuring the initialisation of FTPFS
issue_body:
I have some reserves about the initialisation of a FTPFS object, that come from the fact the FTP connection is not created right away:
* Keeping the password as an attribute of the `FTPFS` is IMO a bad idea for obvious security reasons
* If there's an authentification issue, it will not occur when creating the filesystem, but only after the first use of any `FS` method, causing a `PermissionDenied` to be raised.

So when a user creates a filesystem, he won't see any error until later in his code, which is not consistent with the way every other filesystem work (directly raising a `CreateFailed` error). 

But I suppose it is just a matter of taste, whether to classify an authentification error as a permission issue (could connect to the FTP server but not access to resources since the login failed) VS a filesystem creation issue (we connected to the FTP server but all of the resources will be unreachable, which for me sounds like a failure).

issue_body: Numpy v1.14.x (released Jan 6) changed the algorithm (look for the section titled "Many changes to array printing") for the string representation of floating point numbers. issue_body:
The following is quoted from @zardus in the Slack channel. We should put them in the documentation.

generally speaking:
- claripy abstracts expressions using `claripy.AST` objects
- `Frontend`s provide different paradigms for handling those expressions. The `FullFrontend` (should really be named `SolverFrontend` or something) handles them using something like an SMT solver, `LightFrontend` (should really be named `ApproximationFrontend` or something) handles them by using an abstract (and approximating) data domain.
- each `Frontend` needs to, at some point, do actual operation and evaluations on the `AST`. `AST`s don't support this on their own. Instead, `Backend`s translate `AST`s into `BackendObject`s (i.e., python primitives, Z3 expressions, strided intervals for VSA, etc) and also handle the appropriate state-tracking objects (such as Z3 solvers)
- `Frontend`s take `AST`s as inputs and use `Backend`s to `backend.convert()` those `AST`s into `BackendObjects` that can be evaluated and otherwise reasoned about
- `FrontendMixin`s customize the operation of `Frontend`s. For example, `ModelCacheMixin` caches solutions from an SMT solver

issue_title: DIRAC execute running in a random temp directory
issue_body:
There's an issue in `DiracFile` caused by the recent change to run DIRAC `execute()` commands in a random temporary directory. DIRAC API calls such as `addFile()` are `cwd`-aware and will look in the the current directory for the source files.

This means that `put`ting a file like:
```python
Ganga In [0]: !touch test.txt

Ganga In [1]: d = DiracFile('test.txt', localDir='.', defaultSE='UKI-SOUTHGRID-RALPP-disk')

Ganga In [2]: d.put()
INFO     Uploading file './test.txt' to 'UKI-SOUTHGRID-RALPP-disk' as '/gridpp/user/r/robot.general.gangaproject/GangaFiles_13.30_Wednesday_22_June_2016/test.txt'
ERROR    Error in uploading file test.txt : {'UKI-SOUTHGRID-RALPP-disk': {'Message': 'Local file ./test.txt does not exist', 'OK': False}}
Ganga Out [10]: "{'UKI-SOUTHGRID-RALPP-disk': {'Message': 'Local file ./test.txt does not exist', 'OK': False}}"
```
will fail since the `cwd` is now some `/tmp/...` directory.

I'm not sure that the benefit of running all DIRAC commands in a temporary directory is worth having to manually go through and take into account all the places where the DIRAC command is expecting to be run in a certain place.

My preferred solution would be to remove the temp directory code from DIRAC `execute()` unless it can be shown that there is a real performance impact.

I guess this means going up to Python 3.6 and losing Python 3.5 support.

Right now to add a single read or write permissions, you have to fetch them all, merge the new permissions with the existing ones and send them back to the server using PATCH and If-Match.

While perfectly doable, it's not super convenient when writing a client, no mention of the performances penalty.

A solution could be to add new endpoints for managing permissions more atomically?

The new endpoints could be:

- `/buckets/foo/permissions` to update everything at once
- `/buckets/foo/permissions/read` to add a single read permission (possibly many?)
- `/buckets/foo/permissions/write` same ^
- `/buckets/foo/permissions/create` same ^

With regular CRUD verbs as we know them, and the very same would go for collection permissions.

Bunch of work, and increases the surface API by a lot, so not really sure about this.

Thoughts?

issue_title: pass a func to pmap and pmap_mpi
issue_body:
motivation: pre-process Frame before passing to Cpptraj Action

```python
pt.pmap(pt.distance, traj, mask=':3 :7', n_cores=3, iter_options=new_func)
```

here: `new_func` is a function that modify Frame. Frame would be passed to `new_func` before passing to `pt.distance`. This will make the parallel calculation is more abstract and easier to modify the coordinate and user can enjoy adding new protocol to parallel calculation with minimal effort.

 a proposed example:

```python
def new_autoimage(iterable):
    for frame in iterable:
        # do something with frame
        yield Frame

pt.pmap(pt.distance, traj, mask=':3 :7', n_cores=8, iter_options=new_autoimage)
# this mean: before passing traj to `pt.distance` method, do `new_autoimage` first. 
# use 8 cores for parallel calculation. 
# `pmap` is using python's multiprocessing (vs. using `pmap_mpi` for MPI)
```

given that we have a Trajectory loaded from a list of files with given frame slice.

How to split it into different trajs with correct frame indices?

Hi there, I'm using flask-graphql in a project and got into a question/issue I don't have many clues. I'm using middlewares; this is the minimum example:

```
from flask import Flask
from flask_graphql import GraphQLView
from graphene import Field, ObjectType, Schema, String, relay


class Hello(ObjectType):
    message = String()

class Query(ObjectType):
    node = relay.Node.Field()
    hello = Field(Hello)

    def resolve_hello(self, info):
        return Hello(message="Hi there")

class MyView(GraphQLView):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(MyView, self).__init__(**kwargs)

    def get_response(self, request, data, show_graphiql=False):
        # Low intrusiveness
        result, status_code = super(MyView, self).get_response(request, data, show_graphiql)
        return result, status_code

class DummyMiddleware(object):
    def resolve(self, next, root, info, **kwargs):
        print("DummyMiddleware")
        return next(root, info, **kwargs)


dummy_middleware = DummyMiddleware()
schema = Schema(query=Query)

app = Flask(__name__)
app.add_url_rule('/graphql', view_func=MyView.as_view('graphql',
                                                      schema=schema,
                                                      graphiql=True,
                                                      middleware=[dummy_middleware]))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run(host='0.0.0.0', threaded=True)
```
(The problem happens no matter the version).

If I query "hello()", the "DummyMiddleware" will appear twice. If I query a list of elements, the middleware is executed lots of time, twice per element it seems.
For a query this is _just_ a problem of performance, but in a Logout() mutation what happens is:

- 1st time - the authenticated request is valid, so "logout()" deletes the token 
- 2nd time - the authenticated request is not valid (because the token has been deleted), so it returns an error

Is there anything wrong in my approach to middlewares? I'm not sure which part is responsible (flask, graphql, my configuration...). Any hint would be appreciated.

issue_title: Unable to subclass or emulate DjangoObjectType
issue_body:
Subclassing DjangoObjectType so as to customise it for use in derived objects in the schema causes the execution of:
```python
assert is_valid_django_model(model)
```
which fails the assertion due to`class MyNewClass(DjangoObjectType):` not having a meta class, as it meant to be a subclass of `DjangoObjectType` for reuse later on, not and thus does not have a Meta class of its own.

Its also not possible to emulate DjangoObjectType by copying DjangoObjectType, subclassing the new class from ObjectType, this gets past `assert is_valid_django_model(model)` but then things run into trouble in `graphene_django.registry.Registry` which runs:
```python
assert issubclass(cls, DjangoObjectType), 'Only DjangoObjectTypes can be registered, received "{}"'.format(cls.__name__)
```
Which fails due to the explicit check for `DjangoObjectType`.

It shouldn't require a fork of the entirety of `graphene-django` just to create a subclass of `DjangoObjectType`, either the Registry should be more flexible, or `DjangoObjectType` should be more flexible. 

One option might be to use of Abstract Base Classes to enable a specific set of checks via https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html?highlight=__subclass#class.__subclasscheck__

Another way would be to just drop these assertions and throw appropriate exceptions when we reach the situations they were designed to guard against. If we check that its a Django model to ensure that Meta exists, why not just throw a "Not A Django Model" exception when we try to do something later on that actually needs the Django model Meta information instead of asserting something way up the call stack that gets in the way of other things.

Hi!

The conventional behavior using conan is, each user provides some channel with it packages, for example, I created libusb package at conan.io, so, who wants this package, will look at uilianries/stable. Okay, this works and everybody goes happy to home.

However, if you use conan at your company and want to create some main stable channel, this approach will not fit very well. For example, the developers Foo, Qux and Baz, use the same package in some project, but no one wants to keep searching who is the maintainer of package and where is the real stable package, so, the solution is create a main user name: Company. After this point, all developers know the stable channel will be at Company/version. But, I new problem comes, who is Company user maintainer and who submits in it channel? Any one could submit, since the package was validate (build, tests ...).

The Conan package tools switch to stable channel when it found some pattern in branch name. But, consider the situation where my package will be the main package for all developers, so I'll need bring this package to Company user. To do this, I need to change the user name and password to upload at stable channel. This works, but is a quite manual.

To turn this process more automatic, the user name could be switched by stable pattern, some like this:
```python
# packager.py:490

if channel:
    self.logger.warning("Redefined channel by CI branch matching with '%s', "
"setting CONAN_CHANNEL to '%s'" % (pattern, channel))
	self.username = getenv("CONAN_STABLE_USERNAME", self.username)
	self.password = getenv("CONAN_STABLE_PASSWORD", self.password)
```
This piece will change the current user name to a stable user name, when the stable channel is used. If a stable user name was not defined, so, the current user name is maintained as before. The same behavior is applied to password.

The stable switch for user name allows an automatic switch not just for channel. So, when I need some package, by default, I'll search at Company user, instead to ask who is the real maintainer.

Don't get me wrong. I love typing.

But I think the current two examples (inbound, outbound) of the README are too long.

I guess more could be done in the library.

Why is it not possible to write the implementation of before_http_request() and before_request() in one line?

The goal is to include Contribution and Issue templates so internal and external users of the Python client will best understand how to create and fix issues.

Style guide states, that include should be in this order:

Preprocessor #include directives should be grouped, ordered, or sorted as follows:
* This compilation unit's corresponding header, if any.
* ... blah blah blah ...

In reality, the current common practice is, and probably should be:
* include <openthread/config.h> must be the first #include file.
* This compilation unit's corresponding header, if any.
  * Which may, include <openthread/config.h> instead.
* ... blah blah blah ...

issue_title: Deprecated method tostring() from Pillow/PIL breaks drawable.py

@moonshoes87, @njarboe: Here are a few thoughts I had about the specs for adding reporting to make_magic_plots.py after the meeting today:

- Don't worry about trying to make an output txt file for every plot that didn't get made since that requires knowledge of all of the plots that are capable of being made with a contribution.

- _errors.txt_, _warnings.txt_, _log.txt_ should use the same format as long as multi-line entries are delimited and each entry has a UTC timestamp (possibly UTF-8 encoded text from the Python logging package?).

- _errors.txt_ should only be present in the current working directory after make_magic_plots.py runs if there was an exception or a plot failed to be made even though it appeared the requisite data were present.

- _warnings.txt_ should have entries if make_magic_plots.py didn't generate a plot because a column was missing (i.e. _sites.txt_ has directional data but no equal area plot was made; e.g. http://34.236.167.37/plots/16350/) or a method code wasn't present (if and when it's possible to detect this).

- _log.txt_ should just have info level messages about when make_magic_plots.py started and stopped in the current working directory and what command line programs and arguments were executed.

- If errors.txt or warnings.txt are present in the current working directory, delete them and start a new warnings.txt file with a warning entry that they were deleted from the previous run in case the user isn't aware that this is happening. _log.txt_ should just get appended to until it's manually deleted or renamed.

Just a few ideas to get a discussion started. Feel free to make better suggestions. :)

Documentar todos los métodos de exposición pública con docstrings
issue_body:
**Contexto**

`pydatajson` es uno de los proyectos de código abierto que como _librería funcional_ consideramos un producto prolijo y acabado.

El código en este repositorio tiene que estar bien documentado, bien testeado y seguir estándares de estilo.

**Propuesta**

Vamos a formalizar el abordaje de estos aspectos de a poco, de manera que incorporemos al build de Travis algunas de estas reglas.

**Tercero:** documentar todas las funciones públicas de (a) los módulos auxiliares (los que no son `core.py` de la librería) con docstrings adecuados y (b) las funciones públicas del objeto `DataJson`.

Las funciones "públicas" son *todas aquellas que no empiecen con guión bajo (`_private_function()`

**Cuarto:** chequear que todos los docstring llegan adecuadamente a la documentación automática en Read The Docs.

**Quinto:** agregar a las reglas del build de Travis algún linter que impida pushear funciones públicas sin docstring.
This, according to guys from Celery, should be a drop-in replacement, but suddenly all tests fail :D
Is it possible to use this in a django project admin?

I just did `sudo pip install googleapis-artman`, and then when I try to run `artman --api logging --language java --publish local`, it says:

```
pkg_resources.DistributionNotFound: httplib2>=0.9.2,<1dev
```

And when I rerun the install, I see a line that says

```
Requirement already satisfied: httplib2>=0.8 in /usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages (from google-apitools->googleapis-artman)
```

REPOROOT causes confusion when running artman in remote mode -- on the local machine, it corresponds to a directory that contains both the real googleapis repo and a "local_repo", whereas remotely it refers to a single directory named "googleapis" that corresponds to the merged value of both of these.

The artman config refers only to the remote setup; a path such as `{REPOROOT}/googleapis/foo/bar.py` may refer locally either to `googleapis/foo/bar.py` or to `{LOCAL_REPO}/foo/bar.py`. This is particularly confusing because the `googelapis` in the `{REPOROOT}/googleapis/foo/bar.py` may not exist on the local path if the file refered to is actually `{LOCAL_REPO}/foo/bar.py`.

Instead, we could support relative paths in the artman config, like `foo/bar.py`. This does not to reference googleapis, and so solves the issue of having invalid paths in the config on the local machine.

issue_title: GridSearchCV fails for pipeline with array-like fit_params
issue_body:
The following grid search works for scikit-learn but not ours.

dummy classes for testing:

```python
import sklearn.base

class Dummy(sklearn.base.BaseEstimator):
    
    def __init__(self, param=None):
        self.param = param
        
    def fit(self, X, y=None, **kwargs):
        if not kwargs.get("fit_param"):
            raise ValueError
        return self
    
    def transform(self, X, y=None):
        return X

    def score(self, X, y=None):
        return 0.5
    
    
class Meta(sklearn.base.BaseEstimator):
    def __init__(self, estimator, meta_param=None):
        self.estimator = estimator
        self.meta_param = meta_param
        
    def fit(self, X, y, **kwargs):
        self.estimator.fit(X, y, **kwargs)
        
    def score(self, X, y=None):
        return self.estimator.score(X, y)
```

The setup

```python
xx = da.random.uniform(size=(10, 4), chunks=(5, 4))
yy = da.random.uniform(size=10, chunks=5)

pipe = make_pipeline(
    Dummy(),
    Meta(Dummy())
)

param_grid = {
    'meta__estimator__param': [0.0001, 0.1],
}

a = sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV(
    pipe, param_grid, cv=3, iid=False,
    refit=False
)
b = dask_ml.model_selection.GridSearchCV(
    pipe, param_grid, cv=3, iid=False,
    refit=False
)

a.fit(xx.compute(), yy.compute(),
      meta__fit_param=[0, 1])

b.fit(xx, yy, meta__fit_param=[0, 1])

issue_title: Do we care about k-fold cross validation for large datasets?
issue_body: When considering incremental models how much do we care about k-fold cross validation?  My understanding is that the k-fold bit is an effort to train on all of our data, while still protecting testing data for each fold.  I wonder how often using all of the data is a requirement in the cases where `Incremental` will be used.  My guess would be that this becomes less important.

Would be nice to have [scikit-learn's FA]( http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.decomposition.FactorAnalysis.html#sklearn.decomposition.FactorAnalysis ) wrapped up for use with large Dask Arrays.

issue_title: Investiage changing the default `gamma` for SpectralClustering
issue_body:
The default of `1.0` often leads to 0s in the embedding arrays, leading to NaNs in the KMeans array.

(will update w/ more info later).

The jwcrypto implementation of the RSA1_5 algorithm is vulnerable to the Million Message Attack described in [RFC 3128](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc3218.txt).

A timing attack can be leveraged against the implementation to detect when a chosed ciphertext generates a valid header and padding because invalid headr/padding generates a code exception and cryptographic operations are terminated earlier resulting in measurably faster processing over the network.

Many thanks to Dennis Detering <dennis.detering@rub.de> for discovering and reporting this vulnerability.

Tested using **examples/example.py**.

- enter a multiline command, i.e. **orate** with arguments which span multiple lines
- create a transcript using **history -t file.txt**
- examine **file.txt**

The transcript does not include the line continuation character at the beginning of subsequent lines in the multiline command input.
When does current master arrive in pip repo? The package seems to be behind.

issue_title: Insecure certificates issued due to common name (csr) scrambling
issue_body:
Upon updating from `letsencrypt-auto` to `certbot-auto`, trying to reinstate my certificates resulted in all but one of them being insecure. Upon further inspection:

`sudo ./certbot-auto certonly --standalone -d example.com -d www.example.com -d example2.com -d www.example2.com`

I realized that *all* of my domains have `example.com` (first `-d` parameter) listed as their common name. Thus, causing the certificates to be invalid. Further, deleting the `~/.local/share/letsencrypt/` and `/etc/letsencrypt` directories and running `certbot-auto` with a different domain name first does not correct the issue. I had to completely roll back the VPS in question (thank goodness for DigitalOcean backups) to remedy the issue.

This never seemed to be an issue with the former `letsencrypt-auto`... does it have to do with `--csr` or simply a bug?

$ sudo apt update

$ sudo add-apt-repository ppa:certbot/certbot
$ sudo apt update
$ sudo apt install python-certbot-nginx 


## I ran this command and it produced this output:
certbot --help <--Just an example, but it through a dependency error explicitly looking for certbot==0.19.0. Installed version was 0.21.1.

## WORKAROUND
Modified file, "/usr/bin/certbot" lines 3 and 11, to ignore hard coded certbot version 0.19.0 with 'certbot>=0.19.0' to allow version 0.19.0 and above. 

Manually configured Nginx based on: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m9aa7xqX67c&list=LLMc_sj86mAnw8hN7_LzilFA <--Specifically starting from Step4 at 3:31 into the video until Step 6. 

Made a simple bash script to execute 'certbot certificates' & 'certbot renew' and log/tee the output. Used this for cron job to autorenew certificate every other week.

If one has already setup SSL the letsencrypt options-ssl-nginx.conf will prevent nginx restart.
In my case it's the ssl_session_cache which is already defined in my config.

When letsencrypt-renewer is run, it makes temporary changes to the web server configs, reformatting everything in the process (at least for nginx). Those changes are not fully cleaned up after the renewer is done. Running 'letsencrypt config_changes' after as a workaround implicitly performs that cleanup (`rev.recovery_routine()`) and puts back the original config files. The renewer should be doing this itself.

Note: my use case is letting letsencrypt manage config changes for domain validation, but making the persistent changes to deploy the new certs myself; thus, I don't expect letsencrypt to ever make lasting changes to my nginx config files.

issue_title: Swap partitions feature is not working correctly
issue_body: In order to work correctly there is need to refresh free space for node more frequently, than swapping partitions. The problem is that partition size is changed dynamically right after rebalance, and that means that if there are several swap data events in queue, they will do bad things one after another, because broker free space is not updated after rebalance process.

As we've learnt today, if you specify `-v "add_replica_loadbalancer=1"` or -v "add_replica_loadbalancer=0"` or -v "add_replica_loadbalancer=123456"` or possibly something else, the replica load balancer is created in every case.  To avoid it, one has to omit this option.

issue_title: Front-end failed when kernel is not ready.
issue_body:
I notice that `IPython.kernel.execute` might not already be ready when we execute 
[this line](https://github.com/vatlab/SOS/blob/master/sos/jupyter/kernel.js#L68), which cause the whole frontend to fail.

To reproduce:

1. Start jupyter notebook
2. Create a notebook with SoS kernel
3. From File-> New -> SoS, start a new notebook

In this case the frontend is called but the kernel is not yet ready. The consequence is that the frontend code is not executed, `sos_comm` is not ready, so the frontend cannot receive message from the kernel.

The solution,

1. There is a kernel ready event, perhaps we can do kernel related work in that event.
2. We can move the `sos_comm` registration code before any kernel related code so that comm will always be open. Then, we can perform kernel related work when `sos_comm` receives its first message and the kernel is not configured (e.g. no attribute `orig_execute`.

issue_title: Failed to run Rmarkdown in our docker-notebook image
issue_body: There seems to be some gcc compatibility problem.

The batch mode of SoS makes extensive use of multiprocessing to execute steps, nested workflow etc. Because of the different process model, forking a process under windows is a very costly process. Moreever, because the forked process is new, SoS would have to copy all the data over (pickle), which is very slow. The consequence of this is that sos would run really slowly under windows.

The solution would be:

1. Use multi-process model so that sos would behave the same across platforms.
2. Disable batch mode under windows, which is ok because batch and windows do not play well anyway.
3. Use a single process for all steps, which is basically how the interactive mode is working.

I tend to use option 3 because this would make sos usable under windows with acceptable performance.

issue_title: Remote execution of workflows / auxiliary steps.
issue_body:
It is even conceptually unclear what sos should do for the execution of workflows remotely. The problem is that if we call the workflow with `sos_run`, it would trigger other steps, which might or might not use remote targets. Also, it is unclear how to trigger auxiliary step from a remote step ...

The bottom line is that our current remote execution model is limited to single tasks, not workflows.

The file `python/ray/worker.py` is a bit of a mess. We should restructure it in some of the following ways.

Hi all!

I've got a custom widget with some traits. This widget is basically a live stream, so I'm getting the data into JavaScript directly. I then programatically update a trait with `this.model.set('mytrait', some_value)`. This works great and triggers the corresponding `change:mytrait` event.

What I'd really like would be for a user in a notebook to be able to get the value of `mytrait`. Ideally, this would only do the serialization when the user calls `widget.mytrait`. Right now, if I do that, all I get is the initial default value set from Python (`None`).

I thought `this.touch()` or `this.model.save_changes()` would help, but they did not. So how can I set a model value programatically in JavaScript and have the user in Python able to get that value on demand?

issue_title: kernel / front end communication performance drop
issue_body:
Sorry to be vague, but think I've noticed a significant performance drop in the communication round trip between the javascript front end and the python kernel with new versions of the widget infrastructure sometime in the last year.

For example this notebook

**https://github.com/simonsfoundation/jp_svg_canvas/blob/master/examples/Moving%20circles.ipynb**

(which can be tested using binder) uses tight loops between javascript mouse events and python callbacks.   A year or so ago there was no issue with performance in this notebook but something has changed and now the performance is markedly worse to the point where the features don't really work properly some of the time.   This is one of many cases where I've seen effect and I see the effect in both local and remote jupyter deployments.

I don't know what caused the performance drop, but I would prefer that widgets have performant communication with the kernel even if it means (for example) that you can't have "multiple views" of a widget.  I would prefer to have a single view with good performance.

Has the communication mechanism implementation changed in a way that might explain a decrease in perfomance?

Let me know if this issue is too vague to be useful and I will delete it, but please comment if you have suggestions or ideas.
